Name: Quantum Mechanics I
Professors: Alexandre Souto Martinez
Objectives: To offer students who have completed quantum mechanics, or similar disciplines, in the undergraduate course, a deepening and extension of the fundamental concepts of quantum mechanics.
Rationale: Quantum theory is an essential part of every professional training of physical area, even if the research object is not their main concern. Its importance stems from the success of its application in a wide range of issues, including technology, present in various instruments and devices that surround us.
Content:
1.	Postulates of quantum mechanics
2.	Hydrogen atom
3.	Spin - total angular momentum
4.	Time independent and time-dependent perturbation
5.	Scattering theory
6.	Spin Hamiltoniano
7.	Integration of quantum systems with radiation
8.	Magnetic resonance
Type of assessment:
Arithmetic average of the grades of two tests, with each one having a maximum value of 10.00. 
The following criteria will be used for the final grade:
Grade A (between 9.00 and 10.00) 
Grade B (between 7.00 and 8.90) 
Grade C (between 5.00 and 6.90) 
Grade R (below 5.00)

Name: Physics of Semiconductor Devices
Professors: Marcelo Mulato
Objectives: Introduce the students to the physics of various materials and semiconductor devices, from the simplest to the most modern, such as sensors and detectors used as main and/or auxiliary elements in the development of medical physics equipment.
Rationale: Semiconductor devices represent the basis of modern microelectronics, fundamental to several areas. Currently, both inorganic and organic materials are widely used. Thus, it is expected to be able to introduce students to the physics involved in the operation of semiconductor devices, even giving them an overview of some applications, centered mainly in the field of Medical Physics.
Content: 1. Semiconductor Physics 2. p-n junctions 3. Junction transistors 4. Thin film transistors 5. Metal-Semiconductors barriers (Schottky effect) 6. Optoelectronic devices (solar cells and photodetectors) 7. X-ray detectors for digital radiography 8. Semiconductor Lasers 9. Devices and/or sensors based on organic semiconductor materials. 10. Sensors based on the use of field effect transistors.
Type of assessment: Weekly seminars (P1; written revision work (P2) and final exam (P3). Final grade = (P1+P2+P3) / 3.
Bibliography:
[1] Semiconductor Sensors, Editor: Simon M. Sze, editor John Wiley and Sons, 1994, ISBN:0471546097. 
[2] Physics of Semiconductor Devices, author: Simon M. Sze, editor: John Wiley and Sons, Second edition, 1991, ISBN:0471056618. 
[3] AIP Handbook of Modern Sensors - Physics, Designs and Applications, J. Fraden, editor: American Institute of Physics, 1995, ISBN:1-56396-108-3. 
[4] Fundamentals of Semiconductors: Physics and Materials Properties, authors: Peter Y. Yu e Manuel Cardona, Editor: Springer Verlag, New York, 1999, ISBN:354065352X. 
[5] Physics of Semiconductor Devices, 4th Edition, Simon M. Sze, Yiming Li, Kwok K. Ng, 2021, ISBN: 978-1-119-61800-3 
[6] International journals with recent scientific papers.

Name: Introduction to Computational Neuroscience
Professors:
Antonio Carlos Roque da Silva Filho
Osame Kinouchi Filho
Objectives: To offer an introduction to mathematical and computational methods used in theoretical studies in neuroscience.
Rationale: Neuroscience is eminently interdisciplinary and its subfield entitled computational neuroscience, which has as objective the modeling and mathematical and computational analysis of neurons and networks of neurons, is a research area under fast development with growing importance to the whole of neuroscience. As an interdisciplinary field, which utilizes methods and tools of the so-called exact sciences to study neurobiological processes and phenomena, computational neuroscience attracts the interest of students with varied background from different graduate programs of the university. The existence of an introductory course on computational neuroscience in the Graduate Program in Physics Applied to Medicine and Biology offers to its students the opportunity of having an initial contact with the field, widening their research perspectives especially if their interest focus has some interface with neuroscience. The fully remote offering of the course extends this opportunity to all USP students.
Content: 1. Presentation of computational neuroscience. 2. Basic elements of neuroscience: neurons, synapses and neural circuits and networks. 3 Neuronal spikes and the �neural code�. 4. The Hodgkin-Huxley model. 5. Conductance-based neuronal models and compartimental models. 6. Reduced neuronal models and phase-space analysis. 7. Integrate-and-fire type models and their variants. 8. Models of synapses. 9. Models of networks of neurons. 9. Rate-based neural models. 10. Learning and synaptic plasticity.
Type of assessment:
Weekly exercises lists, containing questions that involve both mathematical developments and implementation of computer programs. The computer programs can be implemented in the student�s preferred language (C, Python, Fortran, Matlab, etc). This is not a computer programming course and students are expected to be capable of programming in a computer language. The final grade (letter scale) will be calculated as below, where AS is the average of the scores (1-10 scale) in the exercises lists:
�	A: 8,5  AS  10
�	B: 6,0  AS < 8,5
�	C 5,0  AS < 6,0
�	R: NF < 5

Name: Detecting and Counting Ionizing Radiation
Professors: Eder Rezende Moraes
Objectives: Based on the concept of the interaction of ionizing radiation with matter, this class presents the concepts of detection, quantification, and processing techniques of electronic pulses from nuclear radiation detectors, including semiconductor detectors and silicon photomultipliers.
Rationale: Whoever intends to specialize in the area of ionizing radiation detection and imaging, particularly in radioactive element imaging system needs to understand the specific technologies and methods inserted in this field.
Content: Review of ionizing radiation sources and radiation interaction; counting statistics and error prediction; organic and inorganic scintillators; photodiodes; photomultiplier tubes, avalanche diodes, silicon photomultipliers, and radiation spectroscopy with scintillators; pulse processing, conditioning and counting; multichannel pulse analysis, presentation of seminars on recent research in the area. Conducting an experiment with a multichannel analyzer to verify the effect on the spectrum with the alteration of the scintillator crystal, an experiment in coincidence detection with the system existing in the laboratory of the person in duty for the class. Study of RC Pulse Shaping electronic.
Type of assessment: Based on a written test and student presentations of specifics topics, graded from 0 to 10, according MF = 0,6 P +0,4 S, where P is the written evaluation, S students presentations. The final concepts will follow as R-not approved if MF <5,0. C: [5,0- 6,5), 5,0 =<MF<6.5; B:[6,5-8,5), 6,5=<MF<8,5 and A: MF>=8,5.

Name: Biochemical and Biophysical Properties of Cellular Vesicles Involved in Tissue Biomineralization
Professors:
Pietro Ciancaglini
Mayt� Bolean Correia
Massimo Bottini
Objectives: The objective of this course is to describe cellular vesicles found in tissues that undergo the biomineralization process. Special emphasis will be placed on the description of the vesicles� composition, biochemical and biophysical properties, physiological and pathological roles, and isolation and characterization methods. It will be also described the biophysical methods used to assess the interactions between the vesicles and important macromolecules such as collagen.
Rationale: Current (bio)chemistry course does not exhaustively cover the most recent advances in the assessment of the biochemical and biophysical properties of special classes of cellular vesicles.
Content:
1.	Introduction: intracellular and extracellular vesicles.
2.	Extracellular vesicles: Biogenesis, physiological and pathological roles.
3.	Biochemical and biophysical properties of biomineralizing vesicles: proteomics and lipidomics.
4.	Vesicle isolation methods.
5.	Vesicle characterization methods.
6.	Interactions of mineralizing vesicles with macromolecules (e.g., collagen fibers).
Type of assessment:
Critical analysis of an article chosen by the professor, with a maximum score of 10 points. Concepts will be awarded according to the distribution of grades presented below: Concept A: Grade equal to or greater than 8.5
Concept B: Grade between 7.5 and 8.4 Concept C: Grade between 6.0 and 7.4 Failed: Grade equal to or less than 5.9

Name: Topics in Biosciences and Biotechnology II
Professors:
Sergio Akira Uyemura
Fab�ola Atti� de Castro
Objectives:
To discuss in a critical way the research projects of Masters, PhD and direct PhD proposed in the various subareas, in the attempt to improve them. To allow students to experience seminars with a view to their introduction to the professor area, as well as to encourage the discussion of ditinct topics included in the field of biosciences and biotechnology.
The program has two objectives in mind: 1) to provide directed and advanced training in biosciences and biotechnology through the analysis and discussion of classical and contemporary texts. 2) to provide complementary opportunities for orientation and research to students in theoretical and methodological subjects, creating the interaction and dialogue among students and promoting the discussion and exchange of intellectual experiences as a support for conducting their master's doctorate and direct doctorate.
Rationale:
The knowledge concerning to the basis of the practice in research is a fundamental condition for the development of studies according to what is expected of a postdoctoral student.
This course comprises the dual purpose of: a) To allow the master's and doctoral students to deepen their knowledge by analyzing and discussing classic and contemporary topics in biosciences applied to the pharmacy; b) To boost the development of their Master's dissertation or doctoral thesis providing the graduate students with the opportunity to discuss their projects and the practical work in progress thus improving its theoretical-practical basis.
The compulsory feature of this discipline is justified by the need for the post graduating students to have contact with teaching techniques and to improve the discussion of scientific topics in this area as well as to discuss the projects under development.
This course will include theory classes based on seminars presented by students in two phases, presentation and discussion of graduate projects and presentation and discussion of scientific manuscripts.
The Moodle e-Disciplines or Google Classroom and institutional email will be used for course monitoring, material organization and student communications.
Content:
- Bibliographical update of the topics related to the field of biosciences applied to pharmacy
- Discussion and methodological basis of research projects from Master's dissertation and Doctorate's or direct doctorate thesis.
- Critical analysis of the results and new insights to improve the research project consequently its impact.
Type of assessment: See Observation

Name: Cone Beam Computed Tomography Applied to Oral Rehabilitation
Professors:
Camila Tirapelli
Christiano de Oliveira Santos
Hugo Ga�ta Araujo
Ruben Johan Christiaan Pauwels
Rocharles Cavalcante Fontenele
Objectives: To provide training to graduate students in computed tomography (CT), which will further enable them to design studies involving CT in the area of Oral Rehabilitation. Initially, basic concepts and principles of acquisition and interpretation of spiral computed tomography (SCT) and cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) will be presented. Subsequent seminars will be held aiming to discuss the application of tomographic imaging in the area of Oral Rehabilitation with critical analysis of recently published studies using SCT and CBCT, which will enable students to become more familiar with and develop critical thinking about the benefits, limitations, and practical use of these diagnostic imaging technologies.
Rationale: In comparison with conventional two-dimensional (2D) imaging, CT presents as major advantages the three-dimensional (3D) visualization of structures and elimination of image overlapping. A new type of CT scan, CBCT, first developed in the late 1990�s / early 2000, is becoming increasingly available and used by professionals from various dental specialties because of its relatively lower cost and radiation dose. Optimizing the use of such imaging is important for increasing the predictability of results in oral rehabilitation. Data from the National Library of Medicine (PubMed) show, for CBCT in dentistry, 8 articles published in 2004 and 566 articles published in 2013; growing exponentially each year. In this scenario, it is extremely important to enable the Oral Rehabilitation graduate student to properly apply tomography in their research projects.
Type of assessment:
Execution of the practical activities of the discipline (semin�rs 80%, exercices 20%).
'The grade will be expressed as A (Excellent), B (Good), C (Average), or R (Fail).'

Name: Hot Topics in Digital Dentistry
Professors:
Camila Tirapelli
Rodrigo Galo
Christiano de Oliveira Santos
Hugo Ga�ta Araujo
Rocharles Cavalcante Fontenele
Objectives: To enable PhD students to critically analyse the new technologies in Digital Dentistry.
Rationale: In the last 20 years, new technologies have been available for examination, diagnosis, planning, and treatment plan in Dentistry. Electronic medical records, digital photographs and radiographs, cone beam computed tomography, intraoral scanning, digital waxing, and 3D printing are among the technologies that are currently applied to dental practice and have gradually become widespread. Thus, it is extremely important to be scientifically studying them. This contact can allow a better inclusion in the job market, opening new areas of research and being a differential in the future of the professional. Thus, the objective of this course is to give the student the opportunity to study these new digital technologies and their clinical applications.
Content:
Week 1:
Day 1: introductory class in digital dentistry
Day 2: Intraoral Scanners
Day 3: Intraoral Scanners
Day 4: cone beam computed tomography

Week 2:
Day 5: cone beam computed tomography
Day 6: additive manufacturing
Day 7: additive manufacturing
Day 8: artificial intelligence

Execution of the practical activities of the discipline (semin�rs 80%, exercices 20%).

Name: Techniques in Radioastronomy
Professors:
Zulema Abraham
Ciriaco Goddi
Objectives:
Introduction to observational techniques in the radio regime.
Review of methods and instruments used in different frequency bands, including radio telescopes and interferometers. 
Hands-on experience on data processing, including calibration, imaging, and scientific analysis of radio data and images.
Observation preparation and design of observing programs in the radio.
Rationale: Observations in the radio regime are used to study a large variety of physical processes (including thermal radiation such as bremsstrahlung or molecular spectral lines, non thermal-radiation such as synchrotron or molecular masers, etc.) that occur in different astronomical environments (HII regions, molecular clouds, supernova remnants, active galactic nuclei, etc.). Expertise on these techniques is crucial for the scientific exploitation of radio observations and archival data from a range of radio facilities.

�	Hands-on activities on data processing with specialized software

Name: Plasma Astrophysics
Professors: Elisabete Maria de Gouveia Dal Pino
Objectives: Most of the baryonic matter in the universe (in stars, galaxies, interstellar and intergalactic gas) is in plasma state, i.e., is composed of ionized gas permeated by magnetic fields. This course shows how to properly treat the evolution and structure of the astrophysical plasmas through a magneto-hydrodynamical (MHD) description as well as the study of phenomena and instabilities arising in magnetized media.
Rationale: This is a basic course for all students involved in studies of the evolution and structure of astrophysical sources and media with ionized gas and dynamically important magnetic fields.
Content: Plasmas: kinetic scales versus magnetohydrodynamic scales. Magnetic Flux Freezing. Magnetic Force. Magnetohydrodynamical (MHD) equations. Ohm�s law for plasmas. Complete Virial Theorem. Waves and instabilities. Parker- Rayleigh-Taylo Instability. Shock waves and particle acceleration. Rankine-Hugoniot relations. Fermi acceleration. Thermal Effects. Thermal conduction. Thermal instabilities. Thermal Winds. Release of magnetic energy. Magnetic reconnection. Origin of magnetic fields. Types of dynamos. Astrophysical applications: cosmological magnetic fields; 'magnetic stars'; star formation; disks and stellar winds; galactic and extragalactic jets; accretion disks; stellar coronae; collapse of interstellar clouds; neutron stars; magnetic field structure of the galaxy; cosmic ray acceleration in shock waves in interstellar medium, supernova remnants and jets; cosmic ray acceleration by mangetic reconnection; origin of cosmological magnetic fields.
Type of assessment: Lists of Exercises, Final Oral Exam; MHD work related to student�s  research project.

Name: Stellar Atmospheres
Professors:
Beatriz Leonor Silveira Barbuy
Marcos Perez Diaz
Content: Radiation field. Equation of statistical equilibrium. Energy transfer via radiation and convection. Model atmosphere. Continuum absorption coefficient. Measurement of stellar continua. Profiles of absorption lines. Measurement of spectral lines. Analysis of chemical composition. Derivation of effective temperature and gravity. Stellar rotation. Turbulence in atmospheres. Stars of different spectral types.

Name: Physics of Supernovae and Compact Objects
Professors: Jorge Ernesto Horvath
Objectives:
The goals of the course are to provide a physical introduction to compact remnants and stellar explosions, with the intention to allow the students understand and (more important) start their own research projects in this area.
Interdisciplinary connections and strong feedback with observational data are emphasized. The course aims to be self-contained and does not require any specific knowledge, since the lessons provide enough material for an ample scheme and a comprehensive study of the literature.
Rationale:
This area of astrophysics has the characteristic to be the point of confluence of several different subjects (elementary
particle physics, gravitation, statistical mechanics, etc.) which are not covered more than in a superficial way in other
courses and related fields, at least in their connections and use for specific problems. There is, however, a well-proven complementarity between the course and the latter. On the other hand, and it comes to a wide area and in rapid development, the absence of any equivalent in Brazil makes it particularly interesting course for interested graduates.
Content:
Introduction and physical tools: dense matter phases; Different regimes at T = 0: neutron drip, saturation of nuclear forces and phase transitions.

Relativistic stellar structure; brief discussion of late stages in stellar evolution; massive stars (onion structure, advanced nuclear cycles and pre-supernovas): 
Low-mass stars (mass loss and hydrostatic contraction).

White dwarfs and type Ia supernovas: white dwarf structure, The Chandrasekhar mass; Accretion and type Ia supernovas. Combustion theory and physical process in explosions.

Neutron star formation (SN II and AIC); Pulsars in globular clusters and original stellar population(s). Gravitational collapse: the onset of collapse and physical processes in dynamical timescales. Why the implosion is reversed? The hydrodynamic shock (prompt), shock revival models by neutrino capture. Instabilities and numerical calculations.

SN 1987A: the lessons we learned: optical and multiband observations; Detection of neutrinos and the 'standard model' consistency; speculations and unanswered questions  Is there a pulsar in the middle of the remnant? 

The birth and youth of a neutron star (a star is born): Timescales of the initial evolution; Pulsars: observations and theories; Composition of the interior and observational tests; The equation of state and its observational support; the measured masses and open problems. Internal dynamics and 'glitches'; superfluidity. Cooling of pulsars. Magnetic field decay in various binary systems.

Name: Inverse Problems in Geophysics
Professors: Leonardo Uieda
Objectives: Introduce theoretical and computational concepts used in geophysical data inversion.
Rationale:
Methods of quantitative interpretation of data in geophysics incorporate several topics of
mathematics, in particular inverse problems and optimization. Knowledge of these topics
provides the conceptual and practical basis for obtaining representations of the Earth's
interior using geophysical data measured at the surface. Tomography methods, for example,
define a type of inverse problem that has found applications in several local and global
studies in the Geosciences. A basic knowledge of inverse problems provides tools for
planning the acquisition of geophysical data, assessing the uncertainty and ambiguity
associated with a model, estimating the propagation of errors contained in the data, and
understanding how prior information about the study area can be incorporated. The
theoretical and practical knowledge gained also provide a solid foundation for understanding
machine learning and data science. These topics are important for the interpretation of
geophysical data and, as such, are relevant as content for a graduate-level course.
Content:
� Inverse problems in geophysics
� Existance, uniqueness, and stability of solutions
� Linear inverse problems
� Prior information and regularization of inverse problems
� Non-linear inverse problems
� Analysis of ambiguity and stability
� Computational implementation of inverse problems
Type of assessment: Exam, practical and theorical exercises, project, presentation.
Bibliography:
Aster, R. C., Thurber C. H. & Borchers, B., 2018, Parameter estimation and inverse problems.
Third Edition. Academic Press
Menke, W., 2018, Geophysical Data Analysis: Discrete Inverse Theory. Fourth edition.
Academic Press.
School name: Institute of Astronomy, Geophysics and Atmospheric Sciences

Name: Urbanization, plans and projects for S�o Paulo and Brazilian cities: from the colony to the new urban frontiers
Professors: Eduardo Alberto Cusce Nobre
Objectives:
The course aims to provide an understanding of the urbanisation process in Brazil as a result of the
specific conditions of the Brazilian society reproduction process from the colonial times to the
present day. The city of S�o Paulo will be taken as the main case study. However, other Brazilian
cities will also be studied, such as Salvador, Rio de Janeiro and Bras�lia. The role of the public and
private sectors and the civil society in this process will be analyzed, as well as the formulation and
implementation of policies, plans and projects for these cities
Rationale:
Since urban space is at the same time the product and material basis of society's reproduction,
understanding the society structuring factors is a key point for understanding the structuring of
space itself. These conditions go back to the historical evolution of this social formation in the light of
the economic system in which it is inserted, which is Capitalism. Thus, in order to understand the
process of urbanisation and the implementation of urban planning in Brazil, it is extremely important
to understand how the country was inserted in the Capitalist System, since this form conditioned the
production and reproduction of the national social formation.
Content:
The course, consisting of lectures, field trips, seminars presenting and discussing texts, is structured
in blocks with the following content:
1) Introduction to the course: urbanization in the Brazilian context: dependent development,
patrimonialism and elite society;
2) The Colonial times (from the 15th century to 1822): the Colonial Pact; Portuguese and Spanish
colonial policies; Planned and unplanned urbanization: Salvador and S�o Paulo.
3) The agro-exportation society (from 1822 to 1930): Coffee boom and S�o Paulo development;
Beautification Plans for the central areas of S�o Paulo and Rio de Janeiro.
4) Peripheral Industrialization I (from 1930 to the 1950s): the basic industrialization and urban-rural
migration; the automobile satellite production, metropolization: urbanization of the poverty in S�o
Paulo; city center redevelopment.
5) Peripheral Industrialization II (from the 1950s to the 1970s): the birth of urban and regional
planning; Bras�lia and the rise of the Modern Movement; S�o Paulo Metropolis: from Avenues Plan
to the Basic Urban Plan 1969.
6) The lost decades: Economic crisis, Globalization and Neoliberalism (from the 1980s to 2000):
The debt crisis; deindustrialization and living standards precariousness; the explosion of precarious
living in S�o Paulo (�favelas�, tenement houses and illegal allotments); strategic planning and urban
legislation deregulation.
7) Recent ultraliberalism: expansion of agribusiness and re-primarization of the Brazilian economy
(2000-2020), ultraliberalism, financialization, and privatization of goods and infrastructure, the
Center-West and the new expansion of urban frontiers.
8) External classes � field trip to the cities areas.
Type of assessment:
The methodology adopted for the development of the course will be based on theoretical classes,
group seminars and individual final paper. The final composition of the grade will be 70% of the
weight related to the individual paper and 30% related to the presentation of the group seminars. To
establish the evaluation concepts, the following equivalence will be used: A (Excellent) = 9.0 - 10.0;
B (Good) = 7.0 - 8.9, C (Regular) = 5.0 - 6.9, R (Failed) = 0.0 - 4.9.

Name: Molecular Dynamics in Apical Periodontitis: Immune Response, Tissue Destruction, and Regeneration
Professors:
Marco Antonio Hungaro Duarte
Flaviana Bombarda de Andrade
Guilherme Ferreira da Silva
Murilo Priori Alcalde
Objectives:
� To understand the molecular and cellular mechanisms underlying the initiation and progression of apical periodontitis.
� To examine the roles of microbial virulence factors, host immune responses, and inflammatory mediators in disease development.
� To explore gene expression profiles, signaling pathways, and epigenetic modifications associated with periapical tissue destruction and repair.
� To critically assess current research on biomarkers and molecularly targeted therapeutic strategies for apical periodontitis.
� To integrate molecular insights into the clinical and translational frameworks of endodontic treatment and regenerative approaches.
Rationale: A comprehensive understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying apical periodontitis and its close association with dental pulp inflammation and infection, is crucial for the advancement of diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in Endodontics. Recent developments in molecular biology, immunology, and microbiomics have revealed that apical periodontitis is not merely a localized infection, but a complex inflammatory disease arising from the progression of pulpal pathology and shaped by host�pathogen interactions, genetic predisposition, and epigenetic regulation.
Content:
1. Innate and Adaptive Immune Responses: The roles of macrophages, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and T and B lymphocytes in apical periodontitis.
2. Molecular Pathways in Bone Resorption: Regulation of osteoclast activity through the RANK/RANKL/OPG signaling system.
3. Oxidative Stress and Apoptosis: The impact of reactive oxygen species, mitochondrial signaling pathways, and mechanisms of programmed cell death.
4. Gene Expression and Epigenetic Modifications: The involvement of microRNAs, histone acetylation, and DNA methylation in disease progression.
5. Molecular Diagnosis and Biomarkers: Detection and analysis of cytokines, genetic polymorphisms, and microbiome profiles for improved diagnosis.
6. Tissue Repair and Regeneration: The roles of growth factors, stem cells, and molecular mediators in periapical healing.
7. Molecular Therapeutic Strategies: Exploration of anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, and regenerative treatment approaches.
8. Translational and Clinical Implications: Applying molecular insights to enhance endodontic therapy and patient outcomes.
Type of assessment: Presentation of seminars about the topics discussed.

Name: The Interrelation of Digital Dentistry with Oral Rehabilitation
Professors:
Ana Lucia Pompeia Fraga de Almeida
Vin�cius Carvalho Porto
Objectives: Enable the student to critically analyze and apply advances in digital dentistry, with emphasis on the selection and use of CAD/CAM materials, virtual planning software, and clinical techniques associated with oral rehabilitation. Promote both conceptual and technical proficiency through the discussion of up-to-date scientific literature, aiming to integrate scientific evidence into technology-based clinical decision-making.
Rationale: Digital Dentistry has been promoting significant transformations in oral rehabilitation, both in planning and clinical execution, with direct impacts on treatment predictability and therapy personalization. The constant update of materials, software, and equipment requires professionals and researchers to critically interpret scientific literature, evaluate new technologies, and integrate them safely and effectively into clinical practice. Thus, this course directly contributes to the training of master's students with an innovative profile, capable of working in teaching, translational research, and specialized clinical practice.
Content: The course will be offered in person. Contents will be discussed based on scientific articles and case studies, organized into the following thematic axes: fundamentals and clinical practice in Digital Dentistry, imaging as a pillar of Digital Dentistry, intraoral and extraoral scanning, CAD software and virtual planning, CAM technologies and their clinical advantages � milling machines and 3D printing, digital facial diagnosis and planning, materials for digital dentistry, digitally manufactured ceramic prostheses, digitally manufactured partial and total removable dentures, digitally guided planning and preparation, advances in digital implantology and periodontics, and digital occlusion.
Type of assessment: Presentation of seminars based on current scientific evidence, critical discussions and contribution to thematic debates, active participation in classes and delivery of final activity.

Name: Body Composition and Health Outcomes
Professors: Gabriela Salim Ferreira de Castro
Objectives: To enable graduate students to understand the relationship between body composition and health outcomes, including chronic diseases, aging, obesity, sarcopenia, and cachexia, as well as to become familiar with the main methods used for body composition assessment and their applications in research and clinical practice.
Rationale: Excess body fat is associated with several diseases, such as type 2 diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, and some types of cancer. Sarcopenia and cachexia, characterized by low muscle mass, compromise quality and length of life. The assessment of body composition is an essential tool in healthcare, allowing us to identify metabolic risks, assess nutritional status, and understand the impacts of lean mass, body fat, and tissue distribution on different clinical outcomes. An in-depth study of this relationship is essential for guiding strategies for the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of chronic diseases, in addition to contributing to the promotion of health and quality of life.
Content:
Body composition across the lifespan (childhood, adulthood, aging, pregnancy).
Body assessment methods and their particularities in chronic diseases.
Metabolic and endocrine implications of altered body composition.
Obesity.
Sarcopenia and cachexia.
Nutrition and physical activity interventions.
Type of assessment:
Individual or group seminar presentation: 40% of the final grade; critical discussion of a
scientific article and submission of responses to the proposed questions: 30%; final written
report: 30%.
The final grade will correspond to the weighted average of the three assessments.
Grade A: m�dia > ou = 9.0
Grade B: m�dia > ou =  7.5 and < 9.0
Grade C: m�dia > ou =  5.0 and < 7.5
Grade R (failed): average < 5.0

Name: Experimental and Molecular Methods in Biofuel Research
Professors:
Igor Cesarino
Chhandak Basu
Objectives:
This course will give an overview of biotechnology thoery and methods used for biofuel production through seminars and laboratory work. The main goals are:  
1. To provide a comprehensive overview of the role of plant and microbial biotechnology in biofuel production. 2. To enable students to critically analyze advanced scientific literature regarding metabolic engineering and biomass conversion. 3. To develop practical skills in fundamental molecular biology techniques, such as RNA extraction, cDNA synthesis, and gene expression analysis via qPCR (Livak method). 4. To train students in analytical methods for plant cell wall characterization, specifically cell wall residue extraction and lignin quantification using the acetyl bromide method. 5. To demonstrate biochemical energy conversion processes, including transesterification reaction for biodiesel production.
Rationale: The global transition toward sustainable energy matrices requires professionals with interdisciplinary training, capable of bridging the gap between basic and applied research. This course is justified by its integration of plant physiology and biochemistry within the context of the bioeconomy, covering topics ranging from improvement of key biomass sources (such as sugarcane) to industrial conversion processes. Offering this content in a condensed format allows for intensive theoretical and practical immersion, where students not only discuss the state-of-the-art in seminars but also master essential laboratory protocols for molecular and chemical plant phenotyping. For the Graduate Program in Botany, this course contributes by modernizing the curriculum with themes of metabolic engineering and sustainability, strengthening the training of researchers prepared to propose biotechnological solutions for contemporary energy and environmental challenges. Additionally, the course will be co-offered by a foreigner professor, expert in biofuels and abiotic stresses, who will strongly contribute with the internationalization aspect of the course.
Content:
Part A: Seminars 
Day 1: Introduction to Biofuels and Biotechnology. Overview of biofuels: Types, advantages, and challenges. Role of biotechnology in biofuel production. Key terms and concepts: 
Enzymes, microbes, feedstocks, and conversion processes. Discussion of a foundational paper on the potential of biofuels and the importance of biotechnology 
Day 2: Microbial Biotechnology for Biofuel Production. Journal article discussions: microbial strain improvement for bioethanol or biodiesel production 
Day 3: Enzymatic Biotechnology for Biofuel Conversion. Journal article discussions: Advancements in enzyme technology for biofuel conversion 
Day 4: Plant Biotechnology for Biofuel Production. Journal article discussions: Metabolic engineering for biofuel synthesis 
Day 5: �Food vs. fuel� debate: Sustainability and Future Perspectives in Biotechnological Biofuels. Journal article discussions:  Challenges and future prospects of biotechnological approaches to biofuel production 
 
Part B: Lab work 
Day 1: Basic gene expression analysis techniques. Introduction to laboratory safety. RNA extraction, Quality control of RNA, and cDNA synthesis.  
Day 2: Basic gene expression analysis techniques. qPCR. qPCR data analysis using the Livak method 
Day 3: Lignin Quantification (Part 1). Cell Wall Residue Extraction from green foxtail and sugarcane. 
Day 4: Lignin Quantification (Part 2). Lignin quantification using Acetylbromide method 
Day 5: Transesterification for biodiesel production. Use of vegetable oil to produce biodiesel.
Type of assessment: Assessment will consist of two components: (1) end-of-lecture tests/exercises with equal weighting; (2) seminars on selected papers. Presentations will be graded on (i) technicalscientific background and (ii) clarity. Both components will have equal weight in the final grade.
Bibliography: Instructors will provide recent peer-reviewed articles to promote in-class discussions.

Name: Cell Biology by the Numbers
Professors:
Fernanda Ortis
Fernando Rodrigues de Moraes Abdulkader
Objectives: To allow the graduate student to observe, understand and use important numeric concepts in Cell Biology, thus aiming at the development of a critical thinking about what numerical concepts such as size, concentrations and rates determine and their biological repercussion. In addition, this course aims to develop the ability to reason about quantitative data in a biological environment.
Rationale:
Numeracy, i.e. the ability to reason on and apply quantitative concepts is extremely important for the advance of the biological sciences. However, this kind of reasoning has been mostly restrained to graduate schools for the hard
sciences, where these concepts are presumably more easily measurable and identified than in the life sciences, in which the recognition of patterns and their reproducibility are more complex. In this regard, the implementation of this discipline is important and timely as it promotes the exercise of numeracy specifically to concepts related to Cell Biology.
Content:
Quantitative reasoning; Space and time dimensions; Concentrations and Absolute Numbers; Energies
and Forces; Rates and Durations; Information and Errors.
Type of assessment: Written exam and attendance to 70% or more of the classes.
Bibliography:
Milo R and Phillips R: Cell Biology by the Numbers. Garland Science, 2016
http://book.bionumbers.org/
Burton, RF. Physiology by Numbers � an encouragement to quantitative thinking. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

Name: Tridimensional Cell Culture Technologies
Professors:
Patricia Cristina Baleeiro Beltrao Braga
Katiucia Batista da Silva Paiva
Marilene Hohmuth Lopes
Objectives: Provide students with an understanding of the difference between two-dimensional or monolayer and three-dimensional (3D) cell culture. To do so, students will have basic concepts about cell cultivation; stem cells; isolation and characterization of primary stem cell cultures from embryonic and adult tissues; generation of inducible pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs); techniques for generating spheroids, cell sheets and organoids; basic concepts of tissue bioengineering; microfluidics; and 3D bioprinting.
Rationale: This course aims to address traditional and current techniques of stem cell-focused cell culture, with a theoretical-practical approach and the participation of experts to give lectures. Considering that the professors responsible for this discipline work with primary stem cell culture from different sources and origins, it is our expertise to standardize various techniques of cell cultivation, challenging to master and very current in applied research, especially in the field of Regenerative Medicine. We believe it is possible to contribute through this discipline to the updating of graduate students already developing their projects in this area through recently proposed and innovative approaches.
Content: Traditional techniques used in monolayer cell culture (immortalized cell line): viability monitoring, cell proliferation and migration, suspended and adhered cell cultures, mycoplasma contamination test. Use of reporter genes for in vitro and in vivo screening. Isolation and characterization of embryonic stem cells and mesenchymal stem cells. Generation of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). Techniques used in 3D cell culture: generation of spheroids, cell sheets and organoids. Tissue Bioengineering Techniques, basic notions of 3D bioprinting and microfluidics.
Type of assessment: Class participation and presentation of seminars.
Bibliography: R. IAN FRESHNEY. Culture of Animal Cells: A Manual of Basic Technique and Specialized Applications, 7th Edition, 2016
Selection criteria:
Alunos que pretendem implementar tecnologias de cultivo 3D em seus projetos de pesquisa. Enviar carta de motiva��o de 1 p�gina para Profa. Katiucia Paiva (katipaiva@usp.br) at� 15/07/26.

Name: Survival and Death Mechanisms for the Interactions of Pathogenic Protozoa with their Hosts
Professors:
Maria Carolina Quartim Barbosa Elias Sabbaga
Ariel Mariano Silber
J�lia Pinheiro Chagas da Cunha
Janaina de Freitas Nascimento
Objectives: To offer the students an updated overview of the state of knowledge about the mechanisms that trigger processes related to survival and death in the interaction of parasites with their hosts
Rationale: The direct interaction between our graduate students and the most relevant researchers in the field who work in international institutions is often hampered by the lack of opportunities, difficulty of access for students to researchers in larger events, difficulties including assistance to these events where these researchers they can be 'approached' by our students for scheduling and even economic reasons. The proposal is designed precisely to fill this gap. Researchers are brought to USP, more specifically to our Department, where they participate in a mini-symposium and stay at the students' disposal for a whole day
Content:
Students will be asked to discuss scientific articles with presenters and/or coordinators in an activity held after the formal seminar. The guest list will be announced in advance and students will receive an article by the same author for discussion after the seminar. Theoretical and practical classes will be held to strengthen learning and provide the context for the different lectures that will be covered in the course. In addition, we prepare study and consultation periods so that students can discuss and interact with guests. 
Theoretical classes: 
1. Pathogenic parasites (discussed in SIMPOSIO) versus host 
2. General concepts of parasite pathogens addressed in the Symposium 
3. Survival mechanisms of pathogenic parasites in the mammalian host 
4. Host response to pathogen parasite infections 
Practical classes: 
5. Period of study / Practical activity (Review and discussion of scientific works) 
6. Study period / Practical activity (Preparation of questions for the different presentations and for the Interview) 
7. Participation of students in the Symposium (preparing questions for the speaker) 
8. Interview with the speaker at the Poster session 
9. Post-Symposium Discussion Roundtable 
10. Preparation of a final report with the full interview for scientific dissemination (in Portuguese and English)
Type of assessment: The evaluation will be done by participation; students who miss a seminar can make up for it by writing a one-page summary (at most) about one of the seminars they attended. Up to three fouls can be made up in this way
Bibliography: The bibliography will be selected for each edition based on the latest works by guest speakers at the Symposium

Name: Anatomy of the Firm in Distress: Contracts, Corporations and Insolvency (Brazil � United States)
Professors:
Francisco Satiro de Souza Junior
Paula Andrea Forgioni
Objectives:
(1) To examine, from a comparative perspective (Brazil�United States), contemporary legal issues
concerning contracts, corporate structures and security in the context of the firm in distress.
(2) To analyze, from a comparative perspective, the interpretation of business contracts in the two
systems, addressing the tension between party autonomy and the limits arising from general
clauses.
(3) To compare restructuring processes � e.g., U.S. liability management exercises (LMEs) and
Chapter 11, and Brazilian (judicial and out-of-court) reorganization � as well as the regime of
security and corporate transactions involving the firm in distress.
(4) To analyze whether there is a shift of directors' fiduciary duties toward creditors as the company
approaches insolvency, addressing the Delaware experience and the Brazilian regime of directors'
duties and liability.
Rationale:
Corporate distress can have profound repercussions on contracts, corporate
structures, security and directors' duties. It is in the context of distress that the meaning and scope of these
institutes are tested most intensely, revealing their structural and functional limitations. Examining the
legal regime applicable to them in Brazil and the United States is particularly useful for understanding the
distinct premises from which these systems proceed: on the one hand, the primacy of party autonomy in
U.S. law; on the other, the limits arising from general clauses in Brazilian law. Despite these differences,
the two legal systems have clear points of intersection. To give two examples, the reform introduced by
Law No. 14,112/2020 drew on mechanisms long established in U.S. practice; and the so-called liability management exercises have given rise to important reflections relevant to restructurings carried out in
Brazil. With the participation of a foreign guest specialized in restructuring and insolvency law, the course
works through these topics, addressing them from a comparative and functional perspective, highlighting
convergences, divergences and the limits of transplanting solutions between the two countries.
Content:
Without prejudice to occasional adjustments, the course will follow this schedule:
Class 01 (August 3, 2026), 8:30 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. Starting point: the main differences between U.S. and
Brazilian insolvency law. Functions of insolvency law and their effects on contracts, corporations and
security.
Class 02 (August 4, 2026), 8:30 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. Restructuring processes in comparative perspective.
U.S. Chapter 11 and liability management exercises (LMEs). Brazilian judicial and out-of-court
reorganization. Financing the firm in distress (DIP financing), plan negotiation and approval.
Class 03 (August 5, 2026), 8:30 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. Business contracts and corporate distress. Interpretation
of business contracts in the two systems: party autonomy, good faith and general clauses. Contractual
mechanisms of protection against counterparty insolvency. Treatment of executory contracts in Brazilian
reorganization and in Chapter 11.
Class 04 (August 6, 2026), 8:30 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. Security and corporate transactions involving the firm
in distress. The regime of security in the two systems and its behavior in insolvency. Sale of assets and
productive units. M&A involving distressed companies: risk allocation and successor liability.
Class 05 (August 7, 2026), 8:30 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. Directors' fiduciary duties in the vicinity of insolvency.
The shift of duties toward creditors: the Delaware experience. The Brazilian regime of directors' duties
and liability in the distressed firm. Comparative synthesis: convergences, divergences and the limits of
transplanting solutions.
In the following week, seminars on the topics of the theoretical classes, with discussion of cases and
previously assigned readings.
Type of assessment: Participation in class, seminar and written paper.

Name: Administrative Law and Social Media: to regulate or not to regulate?
Professors: Vitor Rhein Schirato
Objectives: The main purpose of the course is to analyze the role of the Administrative Law in the age of social medias. Considered to be a free and deregulated space, the purpose of the course shall be analysis of the limits of regulatory intervention under the Administrative Law that may or must occur in the social medias� functioning and structures. Mainly, the course shall investigate if and when a governmental regulation on certain individual liberties may be lawful.
Rationale: Currently, social medias are among the main spaces of sociability and interaction, as well as one of the most important sources of information and news diffusion. However, as it is notorious, several cases demonstrate prejudices to society, and that is the point from which we may ask: is State regulation lawful when applied to liberties exercising in social medias? Based on the fundamental rights, may the State limit social medias? If so, how and by which mechanism? What is the lawful intension for the regulation? All these questions appoint to the importance of such discipline.
Content: 1. Administrative Law and State regulation: fundamentals, mechanisms and limitations; 2. The individual liberties and rights exercising in the contemporary society; 3. Fundamental rights and governmental restrictions; 4. Are social medias subject to regulation for protecting fundamental rights?; 5. Government�s liability in tort before social medias; 6. A Devourer of Leviathan?
Type of assessment: Exams will made based on students� seminars presented during the course, following to the themes previously appointed by the professor, and based on a written work to be prepared by the end of the course.

Name: Temas e Leituras Contempor�neas em Teoria do Direito V
Professors: Ronaldo Porto Macedo J�nior
Objectives:
This subject�s aim is to offer a critical and
conceptually demanding reading of contemporary books and debates in legal theory and
philosophy of law in Anglo American Tradition. The course will consist in a systematic and
profound reading of referential texts for the contemporary debate, with three main purposes: 1 �
Methodological purpose: to present a rigorous and internal reading of some contemporary
theoretical debates, taking the premises of the studied authors; 2 � Substantive Purpose: to present
some of the main agendas of the contemporary theoretical debates, taking a work or system of
ideas as the guide for such study; 3: to offer a comparative analysis between authors and systems
of ideas that skirmish over one of the themes that was chosen as the thread for the semester. This
semester will focus on the relationships between Law and Justice and grounds of legal theory.
One of the objectives of the discipline is to contribute to the Internationalization of theoretical
debate at the faculty, which is why classes with foreign guests are planned.
Rationale:
The tradition of theoretical legal studies
in the Faculty of Law is usually directed towards the study of themes or problems, but rarely to
the analysis and investigation of the specific systems of thought that aim to answer such problems.
The lack of courses oriented towards the systematic and rigorous reading has produced a pattern
of absorption and theoretical reflection that is hasty or flawed by an inconsistent eclecticism, in
which many authors are read, assimilated in academic works and largely cited, but without due
care from the part of the researcher regarding the theoretical consistency of such �incorporations�.
From the methodological point of view, this course intends to offer an exercise of structural and
systematic reading of systems of thought, in first place, starting from its internal premises, and
after, from some of the critics such ideas received from their most refined interlocutors.
From the point of view of content, the main idea is to unpack some of the referential authors of
the contemporary debate in order to, from them, find an entrance door for this same debate,
situating its questions and problems. The course, in this sense, embraces the premise that exist many �entrance doors� for the understanding of the contemporary debate offered by the answers
that diverse theoretical systems give to the problems and questions animated by it.
The shifting of these references aims to allow the students an organized and systematic access
route to some of the main theoretical agendas of our time. It is worthy of note that the very
identification of such agenda of questions and problems is usually one of the main difficulties
faced by students interested in the subject of legal theory and philosophy of law.

diversity of the referential texts to be studied, can be brought the following subjects and referential

Content:
Notwithstanding the
certain variety of subjects treated, the common main thread is related to the fact that the works to
be studied are referential in the contemporary agenda in legal theory, characterized, in a
substantive way, by the articulation of a broad specter of theoretical-philosophical problems, such
as: the relation between law and morals, objectivity and interpretation, conceptions of justice, etc.
Type of assessment:
Assessment will be made through: (1) participation in classes; (2)
presentation of seminar; (3) final essay.

Name: TradeLab - International Economic Law Clinic
Professors: Alberto do Amaral Junior
Objectives: This course, offered in the format of legal practice, aims to provide students with the opportunity to deeply analyze topics in international trade and investment law, as well as broader issues of international economic law, through a combination of theory and practice. Students will gain practical experience in international economic law while also enhancing their knowledge of the subject through participation in weekly classes and seminars.
Rationale:
The course will offer a valuable opportunity for students to put into practice their theoretical knowledge of international law. Students will work on real international economic law projects for beneficiary �clients,� under the supervision of the professor and external collaborators active in the field. The specific legal projects undertaken by students will be defined each semester in collaboration with the TradeLab network and may involve drafting legal opinions on the compatibility of regulatory proposals with World Trade Organization (WTO) rules, conducting research on specific issues regulated by international treaties, analyzing potential diplomatic or judicial solutions to concrete problems, among other matters of interest to the beneficiaries, who may include sectoral organizations, small businesses, NGOs, or government entities.
In-depth engagement with real-world projects will help students develop professional skills through training on how to interact with beneficiaries, optimize teamwork in the face of complex problems, draft documents, and present results effectively and clearly to different audiences. The course is expected to contribute to reducing significant information asymmetries regarding career opportunities in the field of international law by exposing students to real-world problems and, through the TradeLab network, enabling them to interact with experts both in Brazil and abroad. TradeLab (www.tradelab.org) is an online platform that allows countries, NGOs, and other smaller stakeholders to connect with legal talent in the field of international trade and investment law, helping these actors benefit from international trade and investment rules and ensuring that the WTO, preferential trade agreements, and bilateral investment treaties work for all.
Content: Students will work in small groups (�project teams�), under the supervision of the professor and external experts (�mentors�), on specific legal issues proposed by the beneficiaries. Professors and mentors will provide basic substantive law instruction on the respective topics. Students will also benefit from guidance on how to improve their legal research, writing, and oral presentation skills. At the end of the semester, each team will present their projects to the class and to the beneficiary. Potential beneficiaries will be identified in advance each semester. The course will operate as a pilot clinic under the auspices of the TradeLab network.
Type of assessment: Seminars and Project

Name: Design writings: preparing texts for international publications
Professors: Priscila Lena Farias
Objectives:
- Develop skills related to the processes of preparation and publication of scientific texts.
- Expand knowledge about the stages through which academic texts go, from carrying out an
investigation to publishing results in scientific proceedings, books and journals.
- Encourage students to disseminate the results of their research in international publications
relevant to the field of design.
Rationale:
Disseminating results through publications is an important part of the research process. Carrying out
this dissemination in an international context, reaching a broader and more diverse audience, and
establishing transnational dialogues, is relevant for the advancement of knowledge and also gratifying for the researcher. Early career students and researchers, however, are not always
familiar with the procedures involved in the process of preparing manuscripts, submitting and
reviewing scientific texts for international publications. Knowing these procedures better, and
understanding what editors and reviewers of international publications expect to find in manuscripts
is a necessary first step to minimize unfounded fears, improve scientific writing, and stimulate the
wider dissemination of research results.
Content:
- Publishing formats (proceedings, books, journals)
- Choosing where to publish (bibliometric indicators and other parameters)
- Parts of the publication (title, abstract, introduction, development, conclusion, acknowledgments,
references)
- Preparing manuscripts (working with co-authors, preparing texts and images)
- Handling the peer review process (submitting manuscript, responding to reviewers and editors)
- Preparing the manuscript for publication
- Registering and disseminating the publication
Type of assessment: Assessment will be based on the quality of each student's contribution to presentations and discussions, involving: reading and discussion of texts; production of manuscripts; review of manuscripts; improvement of manuscript. Respect for academic ethics, clarity of verbal expression, degree of achievement of the proposed objectives, punctuality and originality will be parameters used to measure the compliance with expectations of quality of work, which will be evaluated as: exceeded (concept A), met (concept B ), partially met (concept C), or not met (concept R).

Name: A materialist reading of contratuality on labour law
Professors:
Marcus Orione Goncalves Correia
Fl�vio Roberto Batista
Objectives: The main objective of this course is to discuss, in light of the epistemological contributions of historical-dialectical materialism, the parameters within which contractuality is placed in labor law. To this end, it will be necessary to demonstrate that labor law constitutes a privileged observation point for the critique of law, since the special role that the relationship between contractuality and will occupies in this area allows us to reach a specific understanding of contractuality in light of the contributions of the Marxist theory of ideology. It is, therefore, a matter of providing students with an original contribution regarding the main theoretical foundation of Western labor law, which is the establishment of contractuality through the identification in the materiality of a relationship between the parties.
Rationale: The subject of this course is central to the understanding of theoretical debates in labor law, and the relationship between the concepts of �employment contract� and �employment relationship� is constitutive of this field of knowledge. Furthermore, the specific focus given to the subject in this course constitutes the core of the professor�s thesis for his habilitation, thus constituting the course as an instrument for the effective implementation of the inseparability of teaching and research, with the impact of the professor�s most recent research findings on postgraduate teaching.
Content:
1. Course presentation and division of tasks

Part I � Theoretical assumptions: a reading of the relationship between legal theory, state theory and contractuality in light of the theory of ideology

2. Ideology in Marx and Engels
3. Ideology in the late work of Luk�cs
4. Ideology in the young Althusser
5. Ideology in the late work of Althusser
6. Ideology and theory of the state: beyond Althusser

Part II � A rescue of the classic assumptions of labor law literature

7. Principles of labor law
8. Employment contract and employment relationship
9. The elements that characterize a relationship corresponding to the employment contract

Part III � A materialist reading of contractuality in labor law

10. Ideology and legal theory: Edelman and Th�venin
11. The critique of political economy and labor law: a reinterpretation of the elements that characterize the employment relationship
12. Edelman's reading of legal ideology and its contributions to Althusserian ideology theory
13. A critical understanding of the reality of the contract in labor law
14. Perspectives for the critique of labor law

15. Evaluation of the course and students
Type of assessment: The assessment will be carried out based on seminar presentations by students, as well as through the weekly submission of problematization questions regarding the indicated mandatory reading.

Name: Corporate Governance Research: Theoretical Grounds and Empirical Findings
Professors: Lucas Ayres Barreira de Campos Barros
Objectives: The objective of this course is to foster graduate students� understanding and critical thinking about academic research on corporate governance, focusing on theoretical foundations and empirical methods. The course explores three of the main governance mechanisms � ownership structure, board of directors, and executive compensation � and their intersection with corporate accountability, financial outcomes, and sustainability. Upon completing this course, students will be able to: (i) apply major theoretical frameworks (agency theory, stakeholder theory, and resource dependence theory) to governance research questions; (ii) critically read and evaluate empirical studies in the field of corporate governance; and (iii) identify research gaps and opportunities in corporate governance.
Rationale:
Corporate governance has become one of the most active research areas at the intersection of accounting, finance, and management. Questions about board effectiveness, executive pay design, ownership concentration, and accountability to multiple stakeholders have generated a substantial body of theoretical and empirical literature, with increasing interest from top-tier accounting, management, and finance journals.
In emerging markets, such as Brazil, governance challenges associated with concentrated ownership, family ownership, and investor protection create a particularly fertile ground for original contributions to the literature. This course is designed to provide graduate students with an introductory exposure to the international research frontier in corporate governance.
Content:
Topic 1: Conceptual Foundations in Corporate Governance Research
1.	Agency theory, stakeholder theory, and resource dependence theory as governance research frameworks.
2.	Separation of ownership and control, information asymmetry, and managerial discretion as foundations of governance structures.
3.	How theoretical assumptions shape governance research questions and empirical predictions.
Topic 2: Ownership Structures and Shareholder Types
4.	Concentrated versus diffuse ownership: distinct governance problems and conflicts of interest.
5.	Institutional investors, family owners, and founders as distinct governance actors with different monitoring incentives.
Topic 3: Board of Directors, CEO Power, and Board Composition
6.	Board structural features: CEO-chair duality, board size, independence, and committee organization.
7.	Board human and relational capital; composition, diversity, and governance quality.
8.	Empirical evidence on board structure and governance outcomes, including identification challenges.
Topic 4: Executive Compensation and Stakeholder Governance
9.	Components of executive compensation: incentive design, pay-performance sensitivity, and relative performance evaluation.
10.	ESG criteria in executive compensation: governance mechanisms for integrating sustainability in performance measurement.
11.	Configurational perspectives: governance mechanisms as substitutes or complements.
Type of assessment: Students will be evaluated based on their ability to understand and critically engage with corporate governance literature, identify research opportunities, and develop a preliminary research proposal. The evaluation is composed of three components: (1) Class participation (30%) � active and prepared participation in class/synchronous discussions, including the ability to draw constructively on assigned readings and advance the debate; attendance at both sessions is required. (2) Pre-class assignment (30%) � prior to the first in-person session, each student prepares an individual short analyt

Christensen, D. M., Serafeim, G.,&Sikochi, A. (2022). Why is corporate virtue in the eye of the beholder? The case of ESG ratings. The Accounting Review, 97(1), 147�175.

Name: Managing for Stakeholders
Professors: Jo�o Mauricio Gama Boaventura
Objectives:
The purpose of this course is to enable students to understand Stakeholder Theory.
Discuss the basic concepts of the theory, covering topics such as stakeholder management, stakeholder strategy, and prioritization model, key-value concepts, and stakeholder analysis. Demonstrate the importance of good stakeholder management for organizational performance. And methodologically enable students to develop a scientific article.
Rationale:
The form of learning assessment:
The assessment will be through the different deliveries that students must make and participation in the class:
The. Individual presentation of the texts, with the submission of videos on the moodle platform
B. Questions about posted texts and student participation during discussions
�. Individual summary of texts with the objective, strengths and weaknesses, methodology and main contributions
d. Article: in which two partial deliveries and one final delivery must be made.

The evaluation criteria contemplating which methodology(ies) used and how the concept(s) will be assigned:
In the case of evaluating the reading of technical reports prepared by the students (one for each class), the comprehension of the texts and the identification and argumentation referring to the main ideas of each text will be verified, totaling 70% of the final grade. In the case of attributing a rate of student participation in class, equivalent to 30%, it is an assessment of a subjective nature, intending to encourage student participation during the discussions.
� Individual presentations with the submission of videos and (10%)
� Abstracts of texts delivered before classes and participation in discussions (10%)
� Elaborated questions and participate in discussions (10%)
� Article: 2 partial deliveries and one final delivery (70%)

Measures that guarantee students access to the platform (classroom with multimedia infrastructure, pro-student room; equipment necessary for student participation and others):
Classes will always take place through the zoom platform and for discussions and posting of activities, moodle, a platform offered by the university, will be used. However, the student must have access to the internet and camera, through cell phone or computer.

School name: School of Economics, Business, Accounting and Actuarial Science

Name: Economic Dynamics
Professors: Wilfredo Fernando Leiva Maldonado
Objectives: To study economic models of intertemporal resource allocation, in particular, models that utilize deterministic and stochastic dynamic programming. To demonstrate the scope of this methodology for the analysis and resolution of microeconomic, macroeconomic, and finance models.
Rationale: Mastery of Dynamic Programming tools and the analysis of dynamic economic models will provide the student with various ways to approach a range of specific problems in Economics. Furthermore, they will grasp mathematical structures that are useful for other types of analysis in Mathematical Economics.
Content:
1)	The Deterministic Dynamic Programming Problem (DDPP). Euler equations and transversality conditions. Bellman equation.
2)	Applications of the DDPP: Human capital accumulation, recursive preferences, seller with unknown demand, and others.
3)	The Stochastic Dynamic Programming Problem (SDPP). Stochastic Euler equations and transversality conditions. Bellman equation.
4)	Applications of the SDPP: Consumption-based capital asset pricing model, job search models, matching and unemployment, and others.
5)	Miscellaneous: Overlapping generations models and sunspot equilibria. Speculative bubbles. Vintage capital models. Error control in the approximation of the optimal policy function.
Type of assessment:
�	Presentation of a scientific paper from a selection assigned by the professor
�	Final exam
�	Class participation

Name: Seminars in Sociology of Sport
Professors: Renato Francisco Rodrigues Marques
Objectives: The aims of this course are: a) To promote an international forum, 100% in English language, for knowledge exchange in the Sociology of Sport; b) To bring together students and researchers to analyse diverse topics of interest presented by Sociology of Sport scholars; c) To promote analyses on specific topics, with lectures from internationally recognised experts.
Rationale: This is an exchange course between researchers and students interested in the Sociology of Sport. Classes are 100% remote, synchronously, through the Google Meet platform, with meetings dedicated to lectures by academics from different countries. Students will have access to lecture summaries and literature recommended by the speakers. Attendance will be monitored using Google Meet's participants management tool. The use of a microphone and camera is mandatory. Students who need access to electronic devices and the internet may use the Pro-aluno room at EEFERP-USP.
Content: Lectures on Sociology of Sport specific topics. The invited speakers are Sociology of Sport academics from different countries. The various topics to be presented will be related to each new course edition.
Type of assessment: Students will develop a theoretical essay in English language, relating their research topic to at least one of the lectures and the indicated literature. Grades will be awarded as follows: A - excellent writing, structure, discussion and approach on the chosen topic; B - good writing, structure, discussion and approach on the chosen topic; C - average writing, structure, discussion and approach on the chosen topic; Failed - insufficient writing, structure, discussion and approach on the chosen topic.

Carmen Silvia Gabriel

Rationale:
Management in health services is essential to facilitate care processes, improve quality of care, and ensure patient safety.
Effective leadership is also an essential element for the quality of healthcare, being among the most critical components that can lead an organization to effective and successful results. The World Health Organization and the Pan American Health Organization understand that leadership is one of the essential policies focuses for investment over the next four years, which confirms its importance.

Name: Clinical trials on Skin and Wound Care: Evidence-based Nursing Practice
Professors:
Vera Lucia Conceicao de Gouveia Santos
Paula Cristina Nogueira
Rationale: As one of the areas included in Stomatherapy, wound care is considered the most advanced among the three components of the speciality (ostomies, wounds and incontinence), at least for advanced nursing practice, specialized or not. Particularly in Brazil, the area of wound care also needs to increase the still limited evidence for advanced practice, through studies with more robust designs such as controlled, randomized and blinded clinical trials. Furthermore, concepts that are beginning to take shape in our midst are those of no pharmachological interventions/ complex nursing interventions and nurse sensitive outcomes. All these themes make up the syllabus of the discipline Clinical Trials on Skin and Wound Care: Evidence-Based Nursing Practice, proposed here. For the development of these themes, we had the participation of Professor Dr Kevin Woo, from the Queen�s University School of Nursing (Toronto/Canada), whose expertise as a researcher and clinical specialist nurse can be seen in his curriculum. Prof Dr Woo has numerous publications developed with researchers considered to be international icons in the field of wound care, such as Prof Dr Gary Sibbald, Prof Dr Diane Krasner, Prof Dr Dimitri Beeckman among others. He also serves as an full professor in the Masters of Clinical Science in Wound Healing Course and has developed quality benchmarks for the care of people with chronic wounds.
Content: Evidence based nursing practice in wound care. Knowledge translation and research utilization. Statistical approach for clinical trials. No pharmachological/ complex nursing interventions and nurse sensitive outcomes (especially for elderly population) in skin/wound care.
Type of assessment:
Students� contribution during classes, being: until 25% - 1 to 3,9, 50% - 4 to 6,9, 75% - 7 to 8,9 and >75% - 9 to 10.
Seminars (with previous articles� readings) which grades are: 1 to 10, being: 1 to 3,9 � worse; 4 to 6,9 � regular; 7 to 8,9 � good and 9 to 10 � excelent.

Name: Global Health and the Empowerment of Nursing
Professors: Vanessa de Brito Poveda
Objectives: The student will be able to identify the most important Global health concepts; understand action plans to implement universal health coverage; analyze nursing leadership scenarios in the global context; to evaluate scientific research about nursing practice in global health.
Rationale: The nursing role in global health encompasses various aspects, among which are the strengthening of actions for the implementation of primary care, workforce dynamics, professional migration, nursing leadership, training, and professional qualification. In this context, understanding the factors that influence nursing practice in the global health landscape is essential for nurses' engagement, enabling a critical analysis of reality and the use of the best scientific evidence for implementing improvements and protagonism.

Name: Introduction to Implementation Research
Professors:
Maria Clara Padoveze Fonseca Barbosa
Carlos Alberto dos Santos Treichel
Objectives:
1) understand the principles of implementation research, its main theories and methods; 
2) develop a preliminary implementation research project
Rationale: Implementation is a fundamental part of evidencebased practices; however, it is known to be globally problematic, as it involves many factors and actors. Implementation science is the academic and practical discipline that seeks to study and overcome complex problems, including those related to population health. In addition, implementation science is a synthesizing discipline that brings together social science and nursing science, among other areas of knowledge. Implementation research emphasizes understanding context, culture, and practices and is therefore particularly relevant to public health. Worldwide, nursing plays a key role in implementation, so developing skills in implementation research will support the promotion of evidence in healthcare.
Content: Block I - Introduction: What is implementation science & synonyms; Evidence based practice & gaps in the implementation; Purpose of implementation science; understanding the implementation research; Challenges in the implementation. Block II - Theory: Theories of implementation; Implementation strategies vs intervention (evidence); Implementation outcomes; Implementation frameworks and models: Determinants of implementation; Theories of change. Block III - Methods: Planning an implementation research project; Potential methods to implementation research � overview; Researchaction; Logic models; Roles within the implementation process; Stakeholders analysis; overview of Joanna Briggs Institute implementation model
Type of assessment: Activity 1) Presentation of group seminars, graded on a scale of 0 to 10; Activity 2) Individual oral presentation of a preliminary implementation research project and presentation of an English summary of an implementation research project, graded on a scale of 0 to 10. The final grade will be the average of activities 1 and 2, with the following grading scale: A - average between 9 and 10: B - average between 7 and 8.9, C - average between 5 and 6.9, R - average below five - failed without credit.
Bibliography: 1. Bauer MS, Damschroder L, Hagedorn H, Smith J, Kilbourne A. An introduction to implementation science for the nonspecialist. BMC Psychol. 2015;3(1):32. 2. Cordeiro L, Soares CB. Name: Systems Biology in Life Sciences
Professors: Helder Takashi Imoto Nakaya
Objectives: This course will provide a multi-disciplinary forum to discuss the application of systems biology approaches in life sciences (e.g. molecular biology, immunology, oncology, microbiology). Students will learn about the latest technologies of the field and will be trained in performing his/her own computational data analyses.
Rationale: Systems biology is an interdisciplinary field that combines high-throughput measurements and computational and mathematical modelling of the many thousand components of a biological system. Using a holistic approach, systems biology provides a comprehensive way of dissecting the complex interactions within biological processes. It has been successfully applied to several life science fields. In immunology, systems biology can lead to a better understanding of vaccine-induced immunity and disease pathogenesis. The challenge relies in dealing with the large amount of data generated from high-throughput techniques and the inherent complexity of the biological systems. This course highlights the recent technological and methodological advances in the field and shows how systems biology can be applied to unraveling novel insights into the molecular mechanisms of biological processes. Part of the course will be practical (hands-on training) and any student working in life sciences can enroll in the course. No computational or mathematical background is required. All activities, lectures and discussions involved in this course will be in English.
Type of assessment:
Fifty percent of the grade is for properly completing the 5 assignments. The remaining 50% of 
the grade will come from aseminar presented by the student.
Bibliography:
Nakaya HI (2013) Applying Systems Biology to Understand the Immune Response to Infection and Vaccination. Chapter of book �Computational Systems Biology, 2nd Ed�, Editors: Elis & Kriete, Publisher: Elsevier
