Name: Current Topics in Physiopathology and Toxicology II
Professors: Patr�cia Reckziegel
Objectives: The course is designed to provide students with up-dated information about the scientific advances in all areas of concentration of the program (Clinical Analysis, Physiopathology and Toxicology) and correlated areas, developed by Brazilian and foreign researches. The participation of the students during and immediately after the seminars aims to develop their critical analysis of research achievements.
Rationale: This course will allow students to interact with different areas of research, making it possible to acquire a broad and updated understanding of Science in the concentration areas of the Program and in related areas.
Content: The program of the course will be defined every semester and organized with topics covering, preferentially, research in the areas of concentration of the Program (Clinical Analysis, Physiopathology and Toxicology) but also in related areas, providing conditions to the graduate students to expand their knowledge on different topics. After the presentation the students will have to provide a critical and short report of the seminar.
Type of assessment: Learning assessment will be carried out through reports on the topics developed in the classes. The final grade will consist of the average of the grades given to the reports of each class.

Name: Ethics and Political Philosophy (Freedom of Expression)
Professors: Evan Robert Keeling
Objectives: To discuss and debate classic and contemporary topics and texts on freedom of expression. and its limits. Some of the main texts: Mill, On Liberty; Herbert Marcuse, "Repressive Tolerance"; MacKinnon, "Pornography, Civil Rights, and Speech"; Waldron, The Harm in Hate Speech.
Rationale: This course will examine the main philosophical debates surrounding freedom of expression. and its limits in contemporary democracies. Drawing on both classical and contemporary texts, the course will investigate questions concerning the relationship between expression, harm, tolerance, hate speech, democracy, and corporate power, providing resources for the critical analysis of central conflicts in today�s public sphere.
Content:
Some of the topics to be investigated are the following:
● What is expression?
● What is expression for?
● What is the relationship between expression and harm?
● What are the limits of freedom of expression in a democracy?
● What are the limits of freedom of expression for corporations?
Type of assessment: Seminars (50%) and paper (50%)
School name: College of Philosophy, Literature and Human Sciences

Name: Meio-Ambiente Urbano
Professors:
Reinaldo Paul P�rez Machado
Gerald Michael Mills
Content:
Content: This course will describe aspects of the urban environment and the climate in particular and place these in the context of a physical understanding of the causative processes. It will also focus on responses to urban hazards and the potential for achieving sustainability at these scales. The course will draw upon examples of urban hazards and responses from across the globe and uses a urban field-walks to link ideas in the course to personal experience of a physical environment that is substantially the product of human actions.
Topics
1. Introduction: Global urbanization, history and status.
2. Urban landscape change and its variations across cities.
3. Urban metabolism and environmental sustainability.
4. Applying physical geography concepts to the urban environment � natural and anthropogenic energy and mass budgets.
5. The urban environment: Climate, hydrology and air/water quality.
6. Global climate change and cities.
7. Urban risk and its management: hazards, exposure and vulnerability.
8. Architecture and the creation of indoor environments
9. Urban planning/design and the making of the outdoor environment.
10. Green and compact cities; High-rise cities; Smart cities; sustainable cities.
These topics will be illustrated using examples from cities around the world; for example, heatwaves (Chicago), hurricanes (New Orleans), climate change (London/Paris) and air quality (Beijing/New Delhi). Students will be asked to draw upon their knowledge of S�o Paulo to place its environment in the context of global urban geographies.

Name: Geopolitics, Geography and Theoretical Strategy
Professors:
Larissa Alves de Lira
Antony Dabila
Objectives: Understand the historical evolution of geopolitical and strategic thought, from Classical Geopolitics to contemporary theories. Analyze the dynamic relationship between geographical space, resources, power, and state/non-state strategies. Develop the ability to apply theoretical frameworks (realism, critical geopolitics, Heartland theory, etc.) to the analysis of international conflicts and cooperation. Explore the spatial dimensions of security, global political economy, and new domains of conflict (cyberspace, outer space, the Arctic). Language of the course: English and Portuguese (translation from French)
Rationale: This course proposes an integrated approach between Geopolitics, Geography, and Strategic Theory, essential for deciphering the logics of power in the international system. In a world of multipolar rivalries, disputes over routes and resources, and the reconfiguration of spheres of influence, understanding spatial factors and strategic doctrines is indispensable. The course will equip students with conceptual tools to analyze issues such as the war in Ukraine, the US-China rivalry, energy security, and the geopolitics of critical infrastructure.
Content: Foundations: From Classical Geopolitics (Mackinder, Spykman, Haushofer) to Critical Geopolitics (� Tuathail, Agnew) and Strategic Theory (Clausewitz, Sun Tzu, Corbett). Key Spatial Concepts: Heartland, Rimland, Land-Sea Junction, World Islands, Critical Routes, Contact Zones, Frontiers. Geopolitics of Resources: Energy (oil, gas, energy transition), water, strategic minerals, food. Strategy and Space: Theories of power projection, strategic logistics, military bases, maritime and air geostrategy. New Domains and Current Affairs: Cyberspace, outer space, the Arctic, geopolitics of pandemics and climate change. Analytical Case Studies: The Containment Doctrine and the Cold War; NATO expansion and Russia; The 'Belt and Road' Initiative; The Indo-Pacific rivalry; The geopolitics of the Suez and Malacca Straits.
Type of assessment:
Assessment will consist of three components:
�	Active participation in guided seminars and debates: 25%
�	Theoretical-analytical essay (application of a concept/theory to a contemporary case): 75%

Name: Primitivos na Gram�tica: Ra�zes, Tra�os Formais e Elementos H�bridos
Professors:
Vitor Augusto Nobrega
Efthyvoulos Panagiotidis
Objectives: Every account of the lexicon posits a division between two major classes of morphemes: lexical and functional. From a pre-theoretical perspective, lexical morphemes comprise roots, understood to encode �substance,� while functional morphemes are understood to serve grammar-internal operations or logical relations as represented in grammar. Current minimalist approaches to the lexicon cast this division in terms of two elementary primitives made available to syntactic computation, respectively, roots and (bundles of) formal features. With the aim of reconstructing the body of theoretical work developed around these two sets of primitives over the last third years, this course explores the core properties of roots in contrast to those of formal features, as well as competing views regarding how these primitives should be represented to account for speakers� lexical knowledge. The course also investigates the behavior of hybrid morphological units, i.e. units that appear to bear both lexical and functional properties, such as derivational morphemes and affixoids, in order to determine what they can reveal about current linguistic theorizing on such bipartite division of the lexicon, and whether amendments to theories of roots and features should be made to accommodate their properties. Generative approaches to grammar that adopt a non-lexicalist perspective, such as Distributed Morphology and the Exoskeletal Model, will provide the theoretical foundation for the course. Students will be introduced to the interplay of such primitives in the construction of syntactic objects and will develop the ability to navigate current literature on the syntax-morphology interface critically, enabling them to evaluate and manipulate these primitives and assess their impact on linguistic analyses.
Rationale: In the last thirty years or so, a great deal of attention has been devoted to characterizing and refining the core properties of roots and formal features. The present course revisits the literature on these elementary units in order to provide an up-to-date perspective on their current understanding, highlighting phenomena that may help refine and advance a fully articulated theory of grammatical primitives. In this respect, the course is primarily justified by its revisionary aspect, as it will provide students with the tools to navigate the literature of a research area that raises numerous questions and remains one of the central domains of investigation in theoretical linguistics. Beyond this, the course also aims to contribute to future research agendas: by offering a comprehensive overview of the state of the art on such primitives and examining phenomena that appear to challenge a bipartite division of the lexicon, students will be equipped to engage critically with current linguistic theorizing, particularly within non-lexicalist frameworks. The course will also explore further research directions concerning the nature of formal features, including their properties, values, organization within the syntactic spine, inventory and universality, and role in syntactic computation, with the aim of encouraging future contributions to a topic that remains comparatively underexplored.
Content:
1. Roots and their forms
2. Roots and their substances
3. Formal features and the structuring of syntactic spines
4. Formal features as numbers, heads as vectors
5. Derivational morphemes: roots, features, or both?
Type of assessment: Final paper - 100%

Name: Usage-Based Phonology: Theoretical and Applied Perspectives
Professors:
Rodrigo Garcia Rosa
Wellington Araujo Mendes Junior
Objectives: To explore usage-based approaches to phonology, with an emphasis on explaining gradience and variation through empirical, data-driven, and quantitative methods. To critically evaluate the limitations of formal and discrete models of phonology (e.g., early structuralist and generativist approaches) in accounting for gradual and variable sound patterns. To employ usage-based methods in the analysis of phonological data, including frequency effects, probabilistic modeling, and corpus-based evidence.
Rationale: Usage-based approaches and their application to phonological data have proven to be a field of great possibilities for both theoretical and, especially, applied investigation within the context of additional languages. This course is therefore necessary within the Department of Modern Languages, as it may provide theoretical and analytical support for all those interested in the description, acquisition, and teaching of phonological structures in additional languages.
Content:
Class 1: Gradience and Variation in English Phonology
- Categorical vs. gradient representations
- Case studies: vowel reduction, flapping, and devoicing
- Implications for phonological modeling 

Class 2: Foundations of Usage-Based Phonology
- Core assumptions of usage-based models
- The relationship between phonological grammar and Cognitive Linguistics
- The role of experience, frequency, and storage
- Criticism of discrete representations in traditional phonological theory 

Class 3: Frequency Effects and Lexical Representations
- Frequency effects in phonological processes
- Entrenchment
- The interaction between frequency and reduction
- Introduction to corpus-based evidence in phonology 

Class 4: Quantitative Methods in Phonological Analysis
- The role of quantitative approaches in contemporary phonology
- Linguistic and extralinguistic variables
- Basic tools for phonological data analysis (Praat and RStudio)
- Interpretation of quantitative results 

Class 5: Theoretical and Applied Dimensions
- Applications to second language phonology
- Implications for the teaching and learning of pronunciation
- Theoretical repercussions for phonological models
Type of assessment: Presentation of research questions (20%) and final essay (80%)

Name: Irish Literature in Dialogue

Mariana Bolfarine
P�draic Stephen Whyte
Objectives:
This course facilitates the exploration of the construction of childhood and youth in
Irish writing. Students will be given the opportunity to analyse texts written for adult
readers as well as texts written for children. The module will examine texts through the
lens of �childhood and youth�, and students will be introduced to a series of subject
areas including myth, folklore, community, education, history, postcolonialism, race,
ability, genders, and sexualities. With a focus on texts from the twentieth and twenty-
first centuries, discussions will be positioned within the context of broader cultural
debates and will incorporate a number of theoretical approaches. 

On completion of this module, students should be able to
1. Discuss and evaluate the construction of childhood and youth in a broad range of
Irish writing from the twentieth and twenty-first centuries
2. Distinguish between �literary childhoods� and �children�s literature�
3. Relate textual analysis to broader cultural debates internationally
4. Develop existing critical and analytical skills, become empowered citizens, and
think beyond the confines of �learning outcomes�.
Rationale:
This advanced level course on Irish writing will be of interest to any students working on, or
with a research interest in, Irish literature. It will offer opportunities for the exploration of a
broad range of Irish texts through the lens of �childhood and youth� and will inform research
and teaching in related areas such as studies in myth, folklore, community, education, history,
postcolonialism, race, ability, genders, and sexualities.

Name: From Cloning to Protein Analysis - Methods and Strategies
Professors:
Andrea Balan Fernandes
Marko Hyv�nen
Objectives: The aim of course is to introduce concepts of all the steps for the characterization of a protein or protein complexes (the gene cloning, expression, purification and structural and functional assays with proteins) using different methodological approaches involving Molecular Biology, Biochemistry and Biophysics and interpretation of results produced with the use of these methodologies
Rationale: Currently we have a claim from students regarding their great deficiency in understanding content and methods applied to the study of proteins, which represent today an important and relevant part of science. This course aims to provide a very thorough introduction to the subject and provide students with the main strategies for the study and characterization of these molecules
Content:
The course content will be taught in the form of lectures in the morning, followed by discussion. We will present all the steps for cloning, expression, purification and functional and structural analysis of proteins. The most used and most modern methodologies will be presented. Students will also receive training in the use of computer programs for protein analysis.
Students should organize themselves into groups to develop strategies for studying different proteins in the afternoon, supervised by teachers. Students will need to bring a computer
Type of assessment:
The evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course through a presentation. The students will present the strategies used for cloning, expression and purification presentation of the methods used to analyze the functions and principal characteristics of proteins or complexes.
The final score will be composed by the presentation (50%) and participation (50%). The required minimum frequency is 75%

Donald Voet & Judith G. Voet � Bioqu�mica � 4th edition, 2010

Name: Physiopathology of Infectious Diseases
Professors:
Alejandro Miguel Katzin
Joao Gustavo Pessini Amarante Mendes
Luis Carlos de Souza Ferreira
Objectives:
The main goal is to stimulate reasoning, critical analysis and the ability to integrate basic
knowledge of Cellular and Molecular Biology, Biochemistry, Immunology, Parasitology,
Virology, Bacteriology, Mycology and Pathology, through the study of pathophysiology of
infectious diseases. The course will be taught PREFERABLY in English.
Rationale:
Representative diseases will be used as examples of host-pathogen interactions and
pathophysiological mechanisms will be discussed.
Content:
Infections caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi or parasites will be chosen as the subjects of
study and used as models to address the main pathophysiological mechanisms involved in
each disease.
Type of assessment:
Method
Evaluations will be made considering the individual and group performance at the oral
presentations and the performance in individual theoretical exams at the end of the course.
Seventy percent of the grade will be attributed from the oral presentations and classroom
participation and another 30% from the final exam/report. An extra exam may be
requested by the student upon relevant and documented reasons.

Criteria
The final grade will be calculated considering the grade assignment described above.
Students who obtain a final score equal to or greater than five (5.0) will be c

Name: Tree Biomechanics
Professors: Jos� Nivaldo Garcia
Objectives: Develop in-depth theoretical and practical studies of the reaction of trees and forests to internal and external forces and stimuli during individual and joint growth. Produce and analyze models of wind-tree and wind-forest interactions, as well as forest management, focusing on the impacts on survival, productivity, lumber yield and the quality of this wood for uses in the timber industry.
Rationale: To train researchers with a broad and multidisciplinary vision, with the ability to produce research in various areas of agriculture, biology and engineering, in such a way as to develop technologies that provide better performance in industrial processes and to think about innovative and relevant academic/scientific production.
Type of assessment: Arithmetic mean of weekly reports, presentations, exercises, tasks and practical works.

Name: Pathology and Microbial Control of Arthropods
Professors: Italo Delalibera Junior
Objectives:
Provide theoretical and practical knowledge of the principles of pathology in order to enable the student to recognize the main diseases associated with arthropods specially, insects, mites and ticks. Present
basic and applied knowledge of the main groups of entomopathogens, the importance of these for pest
control and in mass rearing of insects and mites. Presenting a multi-disciplinary approach to the
different non-pathogenic interactions between microorganisms and arthropods of the evolutionary point
of view. Understand the key aspects of the biology, specificity and epizootiology of the most important
microbial control agents. Study the control approaches used with entomopathogens and the possible
risks of these forms of pest management. To learn about methods of production, formulation types and
application techniques microbial products in the field.
Rationale:
The study of Insect Pathology is of great importance for the detection, control and prophylactic
measures of diseases in insect and mite colonies and it is the base for the use of entomopathogens in
integrated pest management. Arthropods contain complex non-pathogenic communites of
microorganisms that can play important roles in digestion, nitrogen fixation, reproduction, recycling of
uric acid, detoxification of plant compounds contributing to overcome plant defenses, prevention of the
entry of pathogenic bacteria and production of semiochemicals important in the interactions between
and within species. Advances in molecular biology have revolutionized the study of the microorganisms
communities that are closely associated with insects. Understanding the interactions of microorganisms
with arthropod may result in new pest control strategies. Brazil is one of the countries that has been
most successful in the use of microorganisms for pest control. However, the participation of microbial
insecticides on the market of plant protection products is still very small. Training researchers in this
area of science is essencial to explore the potential that microbial control agents present and enable the
use of these in a wider range of crops.
Content:
Theoretical lessons: History of the pathology and microbial control of arthropods. Diseases of arthropod. Noninfectious diseases, symptoms and diagnosis. Pathophysiology. Immunity and insect defense mechanisms to entomopathogens. Koch's postulates. Viruses, bacteria, protozoa, fungi and entomopathogenic nematodes. Diseases of useful insects. Epizootiology. Types of associations and ecology of the non-pathogenic interactions of microorganism with arthropods. Microorganisms in the digestive tract and extracellular symbionts. Endophytic entomopathogens. Theories of the evolution of virulence. Interactions between micro-organism and micro-organisms: viroses of fungi. Microbial control approaches: classical biological control, augmentative and conservation. Use of viruses, bacteria, fungi, nematodes and microsporidia in pest control. Insect resistance to microbial control agents. Production, formulation, and application techniques of microbial products. Compatibility and synergism of microbial products with chemical pesticides. regulatory and ethical aspects. Risk analysis of microbial control agents.
Practical lessons: Techniques used in pathology of insects and mites, demonstration of detection methods, staining and viability of pathogens. Safety in pathology laboratory. Sterilization. Physiology of infected insects: changes in heartbeat of infected insects, pH of the hemolymph and gut. Materials and culture media, isolation, purification and quantification of entomopathogenic inoculum. Koch's postulates; staining, examination and determination of the main entomopathogenic groups. Modeling the dynamics of pathogens and insect. Microscopic observation and isolation of microorganisms of the digestive tract and extracellular symbionts of insects. Preparation and implementation of microbial control projects by students. Virus purification. Basic concepts on production and bioassays with the following microbial control agents: Bacillus thuringiensis, Bacillus sphaericus (bacteria), Metarhizium anisopliae, Beauveria bassiana, fumosorosea Isaria, Lecanicillium spp (fungi), NPV, GV (virus), nematodes or others. Bioassay on compatibility / synergism of pesticides with entomopathogenic fungi.
Type of assessment: Two written tests (weight 2); review paper (weight 1), practical study project with oral presentation (weight 2), divided by 7.

Objectives:
To offer Masters and Doctoral students of the Postgraduate Program in Soils and Plant Nutrition a
dynamic discipline, with the objective of evaluating the state of the art in scientific topics related to the
research projects of graduate students.
Rationale:
The list of disciplines offered in the Postgraduate Program in Soils and Plant Nutrition currently consists
of predominantly basic disciplines, which seek solid theoretical training at the postgraduate level. The
program needs to have a space for the discussion of current and novel issues of Soil Science.

Name: T�picos Especiais em Gen�tica e Melhoramento Animal
Professors: Luiz Lehmann Coutinho
Objectives:
Enable students to have a basic understanding and current knowledge of molecular genetics, genomics
and its applications in production and animal breeding.
Rationale:
The enormous developments of genetic science in its various branches of specialization, among which
molecular genetics and genomics have allowed numerous advances in the knowledge of domestic animal
biology, as well as in the genetic improvement of economically important species. Knowledge of this
area and its interrelationships with other components of animal production is of fundamental importance
for the future of animal production research and applications in the production process.
Content:
Discussion in the form of theoretical exposition, reading, and analysis of scientific papers, preparation of
literature review for the purpose of oral and written presentation, and laboratory activities involving the
following main topics: 1) Genome sequencing, identification of genetic variation, large scale genotyping;
2) Control of gene expression; 3) QTL mapping; 4) Identification of regulatory regions that control gene
expression; 5) Identification of mutations associated with economic important phenotypes.
Type of assessment: Presentation of a written review and a seminar.
Bibliography:
Biologia molecular da c�lula, Alberts, 6a. Edi��o, 2017; Joournal of Animal Sciences; Frontiers in
Genetics, Scientific Reports, Physiological Genomics, Genomics Research; Science and Nature.

Name: Combinatorial Optimization
Professors: Marcel Kenji de Carli Silva
Objectives: Teaching techniques for solving combinatorial optimization problems, especially those that can be formulated as problems on graphs.
Rationale: Optimization problems over graphs and other discrete structures are fundamental not only for their direct applications, but also for providing abstract models on which other areas rely. The techniques for algorithm design to solve these problems are crucial for a solid background in optimization.
Content:
1. The scope of combinatorial optimization and integer programming
2. Formulating various problems using 0/1 variables
3. The Ford-Fulkerson algorithm; max-flow min-cut theorem
4. Modelling various problems using network flows; theorems of Hall, Kőnig, Dilworth, Gale, and Hoffman
5. Totally unimodular matrices and flows
6. Weighted matchings in bipartite graphs: the Hungarian method
7. Minimum-cost flows and feasible circulations: the negative cycle cancelling algorithm
8. In-depth study of polyhedra arising from well-solved non-unimodular problems (matchings, branchings, and so on)
Type of assessment: The evaluation will be carried out through assignments, seminars, and exams. In the first two classes, the course instructor will set the number of assignments, seminars, and exams, the dates for the exams, the criteria to be used in the computation of the numeric grade, as well as the criteria for attributing the final letter grade.
Bibliography:
1. W.J. Cook, W.H. Cunningham, W.R. Pulleyblank, A. Schrijver, Combinatorial Optimization, John Wiley, 1998.
2. A. Schrijver, Combinatorial Optimization: Polyhedra and Efficiency, Springer, 2003.
3. A. Schrijver, Theory of Linear and Integer Programming, John Wiley, 1986.
4. B. Korte, J. Vygen, Combinatorial Optimization, Springer, 2000.
School name: Institute of Mathematics, Statistics and Computer Science

Name: 3D Interaction in Mixed Realities
Professors: Carlos Hitoshi Morimoto
Objectives: This course will provide an introduction to design and develop interactive applications in mixed reality 3D environments. Topics that will be covered in this course are: - Mixed realities: augmented x virtual.
Rationale: This course will provide an introduction to design and develop interactive applications in mixed reality 3D environments. Topics that will be covered in this course are: - Mixed realities: augmented x virtual - 3D perception - 3D input devices: pointing and selection of 3D objects - 3D output: 3D displays and haptics - 3D interaction: object manipulation - Symbolic input, such as text - System control: icons, menus, multimodal interaction - Design issues: metaphors - Traveling and wayfinding
Content: The course will be split into sections to cover the some technologies, methodologies, and human factors that can contribute to the design and development of 3D interactive applications. The course will cover significant parts of the text book \3D User Interfaces': Theory and Practice', and the material will be continually improved with recent papers from related conferences, and case studies.
Type of assessment: The evaluation will be carried out through assignments, seminars, and exams. In the first two weeks of classes, the course instructor will set the number of assignments, seminars, and exams, the dates for the exams, the criteria to be used in the computation of the numeric grade, as well as the criteria for attributing the final letter grade.
Bibliography:
- J. J. LaViola Jr., D. Bowman, E. Kruij_, I. Poupyrev, and R. P. McMahan. 3D User Interfaces: Theory and Practice, (2nd Edition). Addison-Wesley, Boston, 2017. ISBN 978-0134034324.

- D. Bowman, E. Kruij_, J. LaViola Jr., and I. Poupyrev. 3D User Interfaces: Theory and Practice. Addison-Wesley, Boston, 2005, ISBN 0-201-75867-9.

- Selected papers from main related conferences such as VR, CHI, UIST, etc.

Name: Introduction to Stochastic Processes
Professors:
Fabio Prates Machado
Luiz Renato Goncalves Fontes
Miguel Natalio Abadi
Aline Duarte de Oliveira
Objectives: Present the elementary concepts of Stochastic Processes, coherently working the probabilistic reasoning and rigorous mathematical approach through the use of Calculus and Analysis. Present a variety of examples of classic and modern applications.
Rationale: The importance of Stochastic Processes arises from the realization that any realistic model of a real-world phenomenon must take randomness into account. There are a lot of phenomena, in the most different areas of human knowledge, such as Physics, Economics, Biology, Psychology, Medicine, Sociology, etc., where Stochastic Processes are extremely useful for building models. This abundance of application possibilities added to the existence of a rigorous and well-developed theory, with which we can make calculations, are the two pillars of the importance of the study of Stochastic Processes
Content:
1. Random walks.
2. Poisson processes.
3. Markov chains.
4. Branching process.
5. Birth and death processes.
6. Theory of renewal.
7. Queuing theory.
8. Brownian Motion and Stationary Processes.
Type of assessment: Tests, exercise lists and seminars.The final grades will be attributed by the average of the applied evaluations and the attendance in classes

Name: Critical Analysis of Heart Transplantation Results in the Treatment of Refractory Heart Failure
Professors:
F�bio Ant�nio Gaiotto
Fernando Bacal
Fabiana Goulart Marcondes Braga
Sandrigo Mangini
Objectives:
Training and improvement of human resource in research and teaching in heart transplantation, emphasizing aspects related to organ donation, the patient with refractory heart failure and the results of heart transplantation.

Core Question:
Is heart transplantation still the best option for the treatment of refractory heart failure in Brazil and worldwide?
Rationale: Despite the great advance in the treatment of heart failure in the last decades, this clinical syndrome still presents a high mortality rate. Heart transplantation is considered the gold standard treatment of refractory heart failure, however, situations such as the shortage of donors limit the number of transplants and combined to the severity of the recipients, it has been increasing the indication and use of ventricular assist devices. Considering a reduced population for large randomized clinical trials, the critical analysis of the available data and the proposal of new research gives insights and allows better results in the field of heart transplantation.
Content:
The course will be divided into 3 modules and the students allocated in each of them: donor, recipient and transplant. On the morning of the first day, introductory lectures will be held and after the students will have 3 periods of study to prepare the seminars and a specific task for each module. At the end there will be evaluation.

Monday morning � 8-12 am (4h)
Course presentation Lectures:
1-	Situation of heart transplantation in Brazil and worldwide
2-	Strategies for preservation of donated heart
3-	Strategies for indication and use of ventricular assist devices
4-	Early complications of heart transplantation
5-	Late complications of heart transplantation

MODULE 1
Donor � Thursday morning � 8-12 am (4h) Seminars: 40 min each
1-	Critical analysis of the functioning of the OPOs / Transplants� Center
2-  Critical analysis of donor care
3-  Critical analysis of donor assessment risk scores
Group task: Ideal model proposition to increase the number of viable donors - 30 minutes 

MODULE 2
Receptor � Thursday afternoon � 1-5 pm (4h) Seminars: 40 min each
1-	Critical analysis of transplant indications (INTERMACS classification and available prognostic scores)
2-	Receptor: priority vs outpatient
3-	Critical analysis of the use of ventricular assist devices in Brazil
Group task: strategies propositions to increase the number of transplants with better results in Brazil � 30 minutes

MODULE 3
Transplantation � Friday morning � 8-12 am (4h) Seminars: 40 min each
1- Critical  analysis  of  the  results  of  immunosuppressive  therapy  in  heart transplantation in relation to cell-mediated and antibody-mediated rejection
2- Infection and heart transplantation: critical analysis of heart transplantation in Chagas' disease and prevention of cytomegalovirus infection (prophylaxis vs. preemptive treatment)
3- Critical analysis of the results of randomized studies for the treatment of cardiac allograft vasculopathy
Group task: strategies propositions to improve the late results of heart transplantation � 30 minutes
Type of assessment:
Performance in preparing and presentation of seminars and group tasks Answer to the central question of the course
A study proposal involving heart transplantation and / or refractory heart failure.

Name: English for Conference Presentations and Writing Production in the Pediatric Field
Professors:
Thelma Suely Okay
Rodrigo Garcia Rosa
Objectives:
General:
To encourage and guide students in improving their oral and written English skills.
Specific purposes:
English for Oral Presentations
�	Develop students� oral communication skills
�	Improve students� accuracy in oral grammar
�	Enhance students� pronunciation
English for Academic Writing
�	Increase students� awareness of key issues in English writing
�	Address challenges related to translation and adaptation
�	Expand students� vocabulary
�	Review essential grammatical structures
�	Identify appropriate structures for academic writing
�	Develop students' ability to write coherent texts
Rationale:
One of the main challenges currently faced by graduate students is the use of 

English, both in writing scientific texts and in oral communication. Proficiency in these areas is essential for presenting papers at international conferences and participating in internships abroad. This course aims to encourage graduate students to address these challenges by presenting common issues that arise in both scientific writing and oral presentations, while offering possible solutions. This course is part of a broader set of initiatives designed to promote the internationalization of Graduate Programs. 
The course will be coordinated by two professors from the University of S�o Paulo (USP):
Professor Rodrigo Garcia Rosa is a faculty member in the Department of Modern Literature at the Faculty of Philosophy, Languages, and Human Sciences (FFLCH) at USP. He holds a bachelor's degree in Linguistics and Portuguese Language from USP (2010), and both a master�s (2014) and a PhD degree (2020) in Linguistic and Literary Studies in English from the same institution. During his PhD studies, Professor Rosa completed a research internship at the University of Cambridge, UK. He is currently conducting postdoctoral research in collaboration with Cardiff University, also in the UK. He holds a Certificate of Proficiency in English from the University of Cambridge. Professor Rosa was approved in USP�s teaching competition in 2023 and has since been working full-time (RDIDP). He currently serves as a faculty member at USP and coordinates the English section at the Interdepartmental Language Center (CL) at FFLCH/USP.
Professor Thelma Suely Okay earned her medical degree from the University of S�o Paulo Medical School (FMUSP) in 1984. She completed her medical residency in pediatrics (1985�1986), followed by a specialization in neonatology (1987). Before pursuing her doctorate, she completed a specialization in Cellular and Molecular Biology, then earned her PhD in Sciences in the same field at Joseph Fourier University in Grenoble, France (1994). She was approved in a teaching competition at USP in 1998 and has since been working full-time (RDIDP). Professor Okay is an Associate Professor in the Department of Pediatrics at FMUSP, with an additional affiliation at the Institute of Tropical Medicine, where she serves as editor-in-chief of the Journal of the Institute of Tropical Medicine of S�o Paulo. Regarding her English language proficiency, she holds several certificates from the University of Cambridge: First Certificate in English, Certificate of Proficiency in English, Post Proficiency in the Use of English, and Linguistics Applied to Health Sciences.
The course will also benefit from the valuable contribution of Educator Nivia Maria Rodrigues Fernandes Marcello (NUSP 1648602), who works in the English section of the Interdepartmental Language Center (CL) at FFLCH/USP. She holds a master�s degree in Linguistic and Literary Studies in English from USP.
Content:
1.	Lexical study
2.	Study and review of grammatical structures 
3.	Writing texts
4.	Pronunciation study
5.	Oral presentation 

This course was designed to be fully taught in English by means of distance classes (video conference) using tools such as google classroom and google meet, with synchronous and asynchronous content (recorded classes, reference books, recommended videos and links, summaries of topics to be covered).
Type of assessment:
LINGUISTIC PREREQUISITES: having an intermediate level in English and being a graduate student regularly enrolled in a USP graduate program. There will be no test to assess the English level of the students. If there is a surplus of students enrolled, we will give preference to graduate students from the Graduate Program in Pediatrics, School of Medicine. The course will be entirely held in English.

EVALUATION CRITERIA: Continuous assessment through oral presentations, written exercises and individual assignments.

Name: Philosophy of Race and Health
Professors:
Jose Ricardo de Carvalho Mesquita Ayres
Ana Claudia Camargo Gon�alves Germani
Rationale: The background of graduate students in the field of health resents a fragile academic formation in the humanities areas. Most of this contingent comes from courses (medicine, nursing, dentistry, physiotherapy, etc.) whose curricula are progressively focused on the transmission of technical skills and, at best, the achievement of some scientific research skills. However, due to its own character and vocation, Public Health calls for researchers capable of contributing to the solution of problems that involve, directly or indirectly, humanistic knowledge and ethical-political challenges. The severe social inequalities experienced by Brazilian society, with huge repercussions on the health of individuals and populations, with emphasis on the racial inequalities, requires efforts to construct philosophical perspectives and epistemological foundations to support research focused on the racial perspective, in particular the situation of the black population in Brazil.
Content:
Racism is one of the most important challenges in contemporary societies, given that it is, in intersection with other social markers of difference, the basis of deep inequities and rights disrespect throughout the world, particularly in countries where slavery was so deeply rooted in their economic, political and cultural development, as Brazil. This situation makes it crucial to stimulate and bring theoretical subsidies to the study of race and racism from a philosophical perspective. Racial categories shape human lives, not simply by highlighting difference, but also by defining social, political, cultural and health realities. In an effort to understand these realities, this proposal considers philosophical treatments of race alongside concrete social issues to address the following questions: What are the origins of the idea of race? What is the relationship between the use of racial categories and racial oppression? What role does race play in forming human identities? How does the answer to these questions can empower anti-racist basis for health knowledge and practice? 



The graduate course will be taught in 9 meetings of 3 hours of dialogical lectures, with previous readings of about 2 hours each, within the following course schedule:
1: Theories of Race 
2. Intersecting Identities: Race, Gender, Class, Ethnicity
3. Race, Oppression, and Resistance
4: Racism and Colonialism
5. Emancipation, Racial Equality and Health Practices
6: Racial Constructivism and Society (Privilege and Implicit Bias)
7. Identity Politics, Global Dialogue
8. Racial Skepticism, Racial Oppression and the Possibility of Progress and Justice
9. Race, Empowerment and Health Knowledge

When addressing philosophy and race by combining references from hermeneutics as well as thinkers from the African continent, the aim is to expand the boundaries of knowledge and at the same time, foster the symbolic belonging of the participants in the postgraduate program.
Type of assessment: The evaluation of the students will be based on a monograph at the end of the course, dealing with the application of the topics addressed in the discipline (50%).

Name: Saliva: from Basics to Disease Diagnosis and Monitoring
Professors:
Fernando Neves Nogueira
Paulo Henrique Braz da Silva
Walter Luiz Siqueira Junior
Alyne Simoes Gon�alves
Nuno Ricardo das Neves Rosa
Objectives: The course aims to present and discuss the biological, biochemical, immunological, and microbiological aspects of saliva, with emphasis on its application as a diagnostic tool and for disease monitoring. Based on previously selected scientific articles, students will engage in in-depth discussions on fundamental concepts such as salivary gland secretion mechanisms, the definition, importance, and functions of saliva, as well as techniques for salivary collection and analysis. Students will also prepare and deliver seminars related to the assigned literature.
Rationale:
The use of saliva as a diagnostic and disease monitoring medium has gained increasing relevance in both medical and scientific fields, with particular interest for dental
practitioners. Understanding the fundamental concepts of salivary secretion, its importance, composition, and function, along with the different methods of collection and
analysis, provides essential foundations for discussing saliva�s potential as a diagnostic fluid and a tool for clinical monitoring.
Content:
1. Saliva: definition, composition, and function
2. Salivary glands
3. Saliva as a diagnostic alternative � current status and future perspectives
4. Salivary flow, hyposalivation, and xerostomia
5. Saliva collection techniques
6. Saliva analysis techniques
7. Biochemical characteristics of saliva
8. Salivary proteomics
9. Salivary metabolomics
10. Salivary microbiome
11. Salivary metagenomics
12. Inflammatory markers in saliva
13. Saliva and infectious diseases
14. Salivary biomarkers
Type of assessment:
The final grade will consist of seminar presentations (70%) and class participation (30%). In order to successfully complete the course, students must achieve a minimum final score
of 5.0. Final grades will be assigned as follows: A for scores between 10.0 and 8.5, B for scores between 8.4 and 7.0, C for scores between 6.9 and 5.0, and R for scores below 5.0.

Name: Non-Surgical Treatment of Periodontal Disease
Professors:
Luciana Saraiva
Marinella Holzhausen Caldeira
Cristina Cunha Villar
Maria Lynn Geisinger
Objectives:
This course aims to provide theoretical and practical knowledge of the mechanical and chemical means used to control the local etiological factors involved in the establishment and progression of periodontal diseases. Specifically, it aims to:
1. Evaluate the non-surgical techniques used in the treatment of inflammatory periodontal disease.
2. Discuss indications of local and / or systemic use of antimicrobial agents as adjuncts to the therapy of periodontal disease.
3. Understand the influence of occlusal factors on the course of inflammatory periodontal disease and the periodontal changes caused by occlusal overload.
4. Train the student with didactic techniques for oral presentation.
Rationale: The teaching of non-surgical treatment of periodontal diseases represents the basis of periodontal therapy given its fundamental role in controlling infection and inflammation in periodontal tissues.
Content:
1. Non-Surgical Periodontal Therapy
2. Antimicrobial agents as adjuncts to periodontal treatment
3. Periodontal Diseases and Occlusion
Type of assessment: Active participation in seminars and discussions. Final exam on the taught content.

Professors:
Oscar Jose Pinto Eboli
Renata Zukanovich Funchal

Objectives: Develop familiarity with the ideas and methods of Quantum Mechanics and study applications to physical systems.
Rationale: A deep knowledge of Quantum Mechanics is paramount for every physicist.
Content: Historical introduction, General structure (states, observables, symmetries, probabilistic interpretation), Schroedinger equation, Heisenberg formulation, Harmonic oscillator, Spherical harmonics, Spin, Angular momentum, Composition of angular momentum, Wigner-Eckart theorem, Hydrogen atom (� la Schroedinger, � la Heisenberg, algebraic derivation of spectrum), Time-independent perturbation theory, Zeeman and Stark effects.

Name: Statistical Mechanics
Professors: Eric de Castro e Andrade
Objectives: The aim of the course is to give students a thorough grounding in the fundamentals of statistical mechanics at an advanced level, combining an understanding of the main principles and technicques, and the ability to solve the standard problems.
Rationale: Statistical Physics links microscopic theories and the macroscopic behavior of materials containing many constituents, thus allowing us to understand and modify their behavior. In this course, we will study several examples of implementing this program, and these results form the basis of our current understanding of ordinary matter.
Content: 1.Principles of Statistical Mechanics and Ensembles; 2. Thermodynamic Quantities; 3. Ideal gases: Boltzmann, Bose, and Fermi distributions; 4. Non-ideal gases: van der Waals gas; 5. Phase transitions: mean-field theory, fluctuations, and scaling. Monte Carlo method; 6. Transport theory and fluctuations
Type of assessment: Exercise lists, tests, monograph and seminars.
Bibliography:
1. Introduction to statistical Physics, Silvio Salinas, Springer
2. Statistical Physics Part 1, L. D. Landau and E. M. Lifshitz, Butterworth Heinemann
3. Lectures on Phase Transitions and the Renormalization Group, N. Goldenfeld, Westview Press
4. Din�mica Estoc�stica e Irreversibilidade, T�nia Tom� e M�rio J. de Oliveira, EDUSP
5. Stochastic Processes in Physics and Chemistry, N. G. van Kampen, Elsevier
6. Remarkable and recent papers.

Name: Small Angle Scattering: Theory and Experiments
Professors: Cristiano Luis Pinto de Oliveira
Objectives:
In this course it will be presented the basic principles of elastic scattering (first Bohr approximation) and its applications among several experimental techniques as small angle scattering and static light scattering. Several theoretical developments will be presented as well as practical methods and procedures for experiments, data analysis and modeling. As specific objectives it can be mentioned:
� Description of the basic scattering theory by the use of electrodynamics concepts
� Theoretical description of scattering from a system of particles 
� Description of the concept of contrast on several types of radiation (X-rays, Neutrons and Light)
� Description of the concept of form factor and structure factor for systems of particles
� Description of the asymptotic laws of scattering theory: Guinier Law, Porod Law, Porod invariant, etc
� Calculation of the Form Factor for simple geometries
� Calculation of Structure Factor for simple systems
� Learning on the use of several procedures and tools for data analysis and modeling 
� Learning on the procedures for the design and execution of scattering experiments
� Collect scattering data (SAXS and SLS) for several systems. Perform data treatment and analysis.
Rationale: Small Angle Scattering is a powerful technique which permits the structural study and characterization of systems at nanometric scale. The IFUSP have two laboratory based equipment for SAXS experiments as well as two equipment for SLS. Also, there are two SAXS beamlines at the National Synchrotron Laboratory in Campinas. A new syhcrrotron is under construction (Sirius) and recently the IFUSP was granted with a new Multi-user SAXS instrument with special characteristics that make this machine unique in the whole Latin America.  However, the training of students in this subject is still not satisfactory due to the lack of a specific course that approaches these topics on the way that is proposed here. This course will provide to the student the basic knowledge on the small angle scattering theory, applications, methods and planning of experiments, data treatment, data analysis and modeling methods. As a result the student will be able to apply this technique on the study of several types of systems as well as to approach more specific topics.
Content:
1. Introduction: Diffraction and Scattering methods and its use on the study of systems
	Introductory presentation of the course scope, presenting the development of the scattering and diffraction techniques and its impact on recent scientific developments
	The discovery of X-rays
	Development of Diffraction Methods
	Diffuse scattering
	X-rays, Neutrons and Light
Part I: Small Angle Scattering
1. Basic Principles
Interaction of radiation with matter
Dependency of the type of interaction with the photon/neutron energy
First Bohr approximation
General aspects of small angle scattering technique
Similarity among X-rays, Neutrons and Light
General asymptotic laws
2. X-rays, Neutrons and Light 
Definition of contrast in each case
Similarity, complementarity among the several techniques
Radiation Generation and general experimental aspects
3. Form factors and Structure factors
Definition of form factor and calculation for simple geometries: sphere, ellipsoid, cylinder, prism, polymer, etc
Definition of Structure factor and calculation for simple geometries
Approximations for the calculation of averaged structure factors for several systems
4. Modeling and data analysis
Presentation of several methods for analysis and modeling of experimental data
Data analysis by the use of asymptotic laws
Model independent analysis methods: Indirect Fourier Transformation applied for the retrieve of real space information on the particle shape or polydispersity
Model dependent analysis methods: Modeling of the data by using the concepts of form factor and structure factor. Procedures for construction of composed models. 
Ab initio methods and its applications in several areas
Comparison and modeling of data by using atomic resolution models obtained from crystallography, NMR or molecular dynamics (biological systems)
Combination of form factors and structure factors for data analysis.
Part II: Applications of scattering methods
	Presentation by the students of seminars describing examples found in the literature using scattering from X-Rays, neutrons and Light. Discussions on the presented application
Part III: Experimental practice
	Execution of the experiments for small angle X-ray scattering and light scattering at the laboratories located at IFUSP for several systems, performing data treatment, analysis and modeling. Is also expected the preparation of a final report describing the results as well as the presentation of a seminary. On this topic is proposed:
Operation of the data acquisition system
Description of specific aspects for the data treatment on SAXS and SLS experiments
	Normalization to absolute scale
Use of several modeling / analysis methods for the interpretation of the experimental data
Presentation of the obtained results on a report and oral presentation.
Type of assessment:
�	Presentations of seminaries
�	Participation in the classes
�	Practical activities
�	Final report
�	Written and oral exams
�	Aproval: Final grade 
Bibliography:

2. Glatter, O. & Kratky, O. (1982). Small Angle X-ray Scattering. Academic Press, London.
3. Gomez, M. A. (2009). Applications of Synchrotron Light to Scattering and Diffraction in Materials and Life Sciences 776, 1-314.

Name: Quantum Information and Quantum Noise
Professors:
Marcelo Martinelli
Barbara Lopes Amaral
Objectives: The main goal of this course is to introduce the student to current topics in the field of quantum information theory as well as potential applications
Rationale:
Quantum information and correlated areas constitutes one of the most active fields of research in physics. Part of this interest is motivated by new applications, such as quantum computing and quantum communications. 
The other motivation comes from applications of these concepts to areas such as condensed matter physics, quantum optics and high energy physics. The goal of this course is to introduce the basic language used in this field of research and motivate diverse applications. In my view, this course is complementary to the general development of the student, allowing 
him/her to explore more deeply other properties of quantum mechanics, which are not explored in conventional courses.
Content:
1. Formal framework of Quantum Mechanics 
1.1.Linear algebra review; algebra of operators.
1.2.One qubit and applications: the BB84 protocol.
1.3.Two qubits, multipartite systems and tensor products.
1.4.Entanglement and applications: the Ekert91 protocol. 
2.Density matrix theory: 
2.1.Definition and properties of the density matrix.  
2.2.Density matrices from entanglement. 
2.3.Partial trace and reduced density matrices.  
2.4.Generalized measurements and POVMs.  
2.5.An elementary introduction to quantum channels.
3.Quantum Entanglement: 
3.1.Von Neumann Entropy and relative entropy.  
3.2.Entanglement and properties.  
3.3.Local Operations and Classical Communication. 
3.4.Entanglement quantifiers.  
3.5.Applications: quantum teleportation, superdense coding, quantum communication, quantum cryptography.
4.Quantum nonlocality and contextuality: 
4.1.Nonlocality, contextuality and the problem of �hidden variable� in quantum mechanics. 
4.2.Applications of nonlocality: random number generation,  certification of quantum devices and self-testing.  
4.3.Applications of contextuality: random number generation, quantum computation based on �magic state� distillation, measurement based quantum computation.
5.Quantum Computation.  
5.1.Models of quantum computation. 
5.2.Quantum Circuits. 
5.3.Quantum Fourier Transform.  
5.4.Shor�s algorithm.  
5.5.Quantum error correction. 
5.6.Quantum computers and quantum supremacy.
Type of assessment: List of problems and seminars (depending on the number of students).
Bibliography:
Main Texts: 
� M. A. Nielsen and I. L. Chuang, Quantum Computation and Quantum Information. Cambridge University Press 2010.
� M. M. Wilde, Quantum Information Theory. Cambridge University Press 2013. 
� D. Bruss and G. Leuchs, Lectures on Quantum Information, Wiley-VCH 2007. 
� K. Jacobs, Quantum measurement Theory and its applications, Cambridge University Press 2014.  
Other Texts:
� M. Terra Cunha, No��es de Informa��o Qu�ntica, IMPA 2011.  
� B. Amaral and M. Terra Cunha, On Graph Approaches to Contextuality and Their Role in Quantum Theory, Springer 2018.
Selection criteria: PREREQUISITES:  Quantum Mechanics 1 (graduate or undergraduate).

Name: Astroparticle Physics
Professors: Edivaldo Moura Santos
Objectives: This course is intended as an introduction to astroparticle physics, both from theorectical as from experimental perspectives. The student should learn the basic theory behind each topic as well as their experimental signals and detectability.
Rationale: Astroparticle physics is a contemporary field, with intriguing challenges. Dark matter is an example of a current quest to understand the composition of the Universe. The other syllabus topics include both challenges on their own or serve as important messengers of astroparticle physics phenomena. Therefore it should be included in a physics graduate program.
Content:
The observation of cosmological and astrophysical phenomena is one of the most promising ways to understand the fundamental laws of nature. In this course we will show the deep connection between particle physics and cosmology, discussing phenomena (dark matter, neutrinos, strong CP problem and matter/antimatter asymmetry) in which the interplay is particularly intriguing. It will be ministrated in English. More in detail, the syllabus of the lectures is as follows:
SYLLABUS:
�Preliminaries: review of the Standard Model of Particle Physics and of the Standard Cosmological Model;
�Cosmic Rays
�Neutrinos
�Dark Matter
�Matter/Anti-Matter Asymmetry
Type of assessment: Homework assignments and seminars (or Oral examinations).

Name: From Differential Geometry to General Relativity
Professors:
Luis Raul Weber Abramo
Daniel Lopez Cano
Objectives: The main goal of the course is to introduce, in an accessible yet rigorous way, the foundations of differential geometry and to show how this mathematical language directly connects to the modern formulation of General Relativity. Students are expected to: ● Acquire a unified view of fundamental mathematical concepts (vector spaces, tensors, connections, metrics); ● Understand the geometric structure underlying General Relativity; ● Develop both theoretical formalism and practical intuition through guided exercises and physical examples.
Rationale: A significant number of graduate students begin their master�s or doctoral studies without formal training in differential geometry. Although this knowledge is not essential for certain applied areas, such as numerical cosmology, it constitutes the conceptual foundation of several fundamental physical theories, including classical field theory and General Relativity. By offering an intensive and introductory course, this discipline aims to fill that gap, providing students with the mathematical and conceptual tools necessary to deepen their understanding of modern theoretical physics and to prepare the ground for advanced studies in gravitation and cosmology.
Content:
The course will be structured in thematic blocks, each consisting of a theoretical lecture (2h) and a practical class (1h), to consolidate both the mathematical foundations and their application in examples and exercises:
● Vector Spaces: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● Topological Manifolds: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● Tangent and Cotangent Spaces: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● Vector, Covector, and Tensor Fields: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● Covariant Derivative: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● Metric Structure: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● Integrals on Manifolds: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● Newtonian Gravitation: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h) 
● General Relativity: Lecture (2h) + Practice (1h)
Type of assessment: The evaluation will consist of two exams: the first, given after the lecture on Vector, Covector, and Tensor Fields, worth 40%, and the final exam, worth 60%. If the grade obtained using only the final exam is higher than the weighted average of both exams, the final exam grade will be taken as the course grade.
Bibliography:
Main texts:  
Daniel L�pez Cano, From Differential Geometry to General Relativity, Lecture Notes (in prep.).
Other Texts: 
● Frederic P. Schuller, Lectures on Geometric Foundations of Physics, available at: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLmsIjFudc1l2wDQ_ekx6iLtqcWJQQvOsw 
● Richie Dadley, Notes on Geometric Foundations of Physics, available at: https://richie291.wixsite.com/theoreticalphysics/all-notes
Selection criteria: PREREQUISITES: Linear algebra, multivariable calculus, differential equations.

Name: Frustrated Magnetism
Professors: Eric de Castro e Andrade
Objectives: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the field from a primarily theoretical and conceptual perspective, while maintaining close contact with experimental realizations. Students will be introduced to core models, analytical and numerical methods, and selected advanced topics at the forefront of current research.
Rationale: Frustrated magnetism is one of the most dynamic and interdisciplinary areas of modern condensed matter physics. Frustration arises when competing interactions in a many-body system cannot be simultaneously satisfied, leading to a highly degenerate manifold of states. A paradigmatic example is the antiferromagnetic Ising model on a triangular lattice. Such frustration suppresses conventional order and gives rise to a wealth of emergent phases and phenomena, including unconventional magnetic states, spin liquids, and fractionalized excitations with deep connections to topological matter.
Type of assessment: EVALUATION CRITERIA: Exercise lists, tests, monographs, and seminars.
Bibliography:

C. Lacroix et al. (Ed), Introduction to Frustrated Magnetism
S. Sachdev, Quantum Phases of Matter
A. Auerbach, Interacting Electrons and Quantum Magnetism
K. Yosida, Theory of Magnetism

Name: Biomechatronics and Biorobotics
Professors: Arturo Forner Cordero
Objectives: To introduce biologically inspired robotic systems. To provide, at a graduate level, the concepts, theories, and applications of mechanics, electronics, and control theory for the study of biological movement. To provide the basic concepts for the development of robotic systems for physical therapy and functional rehabilitation. To present the concepts of biomimetic engineering design applied to robotics.
Rationale: The study of biological movement systems serves as a foundation for the development of mechatronic systems for human support (rehabilitation and assistance), for the development of new concepts in robotics and mechatronics (biomimetics), and for improving scientific knowledge of the nervous system. Bio-inspiration has applications in the fields of computing, artificial intelligence, control, instrumentation, sensors, and mechanical systems, among other related disciplines. This course aims to familiarize students with such systems, highlighting the most relevant concepts, empirically proven techniques, and current research trends.
Content:
1. Introduction
2. Basic concepts of anatomy and mechanical modeling of the human body
3. Review of Forward and Inverse Kinematics for Robotic Manipulators
4. Human body sensors and sensors in Biorobotics
5. Biological actuators. The muscle
6. Biologically Inspired Control Architectures
7. The CPG (Central Pattern Generator) as a model for cyclic movement control
8. Description and modeling of biological motor control
9. Artificial compensation of neuromotor impairments
10. Bio-inspired robotic exoskeletons: Upper limbs
11. Bio-inspired robotic exoskeletons: Lower limbs
12. Bio-inspired legged robots

Name: Sensors, Actuators, and Bayesian Inverse Problems in Medicine
Professors:
Fabio Gagliardi Cozman
Marcos de Sales Guerra Tsuzuki
Ra�l Gonz�lez Lima
Objectives: Describe and analyze the physical principles and technologies of sensors and actuators used in medicine, including those that are designed by mechatronics and nanotechnology-based principles. Frequently the magnitudes which can be measured are not interesting in medicine. Other magnitudes of interest in medicine can be indirectly estimated, defining the inverse problems. In this discipline, the basic principles of inverse problem solving, the Bayes' probabilities analysis, with a focus on medical imaging, segmentation techniques and image registration will be taught.
Rationale: A comprehensive view of the physical principles and technologies underlying the sensors and actuators in medicine and the statistical principles used in the generation of medical images is required by professionals in the exact sciences who work closely with professionals in the field of human health. Common language, context in medicine, physiology, physics and technology are necessary ingredients in research in biomedical and biomechanical engineering.
Type of assessment:
Work
Examination: oral presentation of the work and questioning about its most important topics.
Average: (work grade + examination grade) / 2

Name: Climate Justice
Professors: Danielle Hanna Rached
Objectives:
To develop critical thinking about climate justice. Climate change has come to encompass the unequal distribution of impacts on a variety of groups, including indigenous peoples, women, youth and activists from across the Global South
To understand key concepts related to the topic, such as the principle of common but differentiated responsibilities, equity, intergenerational rights, nationally determined contributions).
To study and analyze what happens to social movement claims when they reach international courts.
Rationale: As the �Climate Justice� course is built around three axes: the international, the sociological and the normative, it embraces the complexity of the topic by weaving interdisciplinary thinking (governance, law and sociology), allowing a broad exploration of what climate emergency means at the international level.
Content:
The discourse about climate justice is omnipresent in contemporary law and politics.
Representatives of states, of social movements and political theorists have been invoking the term to explain how the lens of the climate emergency has altered the demands traditionally made in the name of social justice. On these occasions, it is possible to notice that the essence of the concept might differ. Given the methodological impossibility of a single approach to understanding the term, the objective of this course is to discuss the concept of climate justice in three dimensions: the international; the sociological, and the normative. What are the main points of conflict between the three dimensions? What can we add to the general theory of justice based on the new demands of social movements and in accordance to international climate obligations?

Name: Scientific Writing and Paper Development for International Publications
Professors:
Eduardo de Senzi Zancul
Marly Monteiro de Carvalho
Roberta de Castro Souza Piao
Tiago Fonseca Albuquerque Cavalcanti Sigahi
Thayla Tavares de Sousa Zomer
Objectives:
The goal of this course is to empower students with advanced competencies in scientific writing and research
dissemination, enabling them to produce high-caliber manuscripts for leading international journals. By completing
this course, students will be able to:
� Develop research manuscripts that meet the highest standards of clarity, rigor, and scholarly impact, suitable for
submission to top-tier journals.
� Master advanced writing techniques, ensuring their work communicates complex ideas with precision, coherence,
and logical flow.
� Conduct comprehensive, critical literature reviews that reveal significant research gaps and justify the relevance of
their study.
� Formulate and substantiate compelling research arguments that clearly articulate their contribution to the field.
� Internalize and apply the ethical principles critical to responsible research and publication, including data integrity,
authorship standards, and conflict of interest management.
� Gain hands-on experience with the peer review process, developing the skills to respond to reviewers' feedback
constructively and effectively revise manuscripts.
This course positions students not only to publish but to do so with authority, ethical integrity, and impact, establishing
their work as a credible and valuable contribution to global scholarly conversations in production engineering.
Rationale:
This course addresses a critical need for students to effectively communicate their research in academic settings, as
publication in high-quality journals is essential for advancing knowledge and professional recognition. Scientific writing
skills are indispensable for making research accessible, impactful, and widely disseminated. Additionally, the course
emphasizes ethical standards, helping students navigate common challenges in publishing, such as plagiarism, data
integrity, and peer review engagement. By guiding students through the entire process of manuscript preparation, from
literature review to final submission, the course supports their academic development. The course offers a structured
approach to mastering these skills, with a mix of theoretical learning and practical application, preparing students to
contribute meaningfully to their fields and enhance the visibility and credibility of their research in international
academic circles.
Content:
Week 1: Introduction to scientific writing in production engineering: Purpose and principles of scientific writing, writing
for an academic audience, characteristics of high-impact research papers, overview of the publication process,
discussion on types of papers.
Week 2: Identifying a research question and framing the study: Finding research gaps in the field, crafting a
compelling research question, theoretical frameworks and research contributions, writing the introduction section:
problem statement, objectives and contributions of the article.
Week 3: Conducting and writing a literature review/background: approaches to literature searching and organization,
synthesizing and critically analyzing prior research, structuring the literature review, common pitfalls in literature review
writing.
Week 4: Research methodology and design: overview of quantitative and qualitative methods in Production Engineering,
ensuring rigor and reproducibility, data collection, analysis, and presentation, writing the methods section clearly and
concisely.
Week 5: Results and discussion: Presenting results effectively (tables, figures, and narrative), writing the results
section: clarity and conciseness, crafting a discussion section: interpreting findings and implications, linking results to
the research question and literature.
Week 6: Argumentation and contribution statement & writing the conclusion and abstract: Making a clear argument:
logic, evidence, and coherence, stating the research contribution, articulating theoretical and practical implications and
writing the conclusion and abstract: summarizing key findings and contributions, writing a concise, informative
abstract, crafting effective titles and keywords
Week 7: Ethical standards in scientific publishing & peer review process and manuscript revision: understanding
plagiarism and how to avoid it, data integrity and ethical reporting of research, authorship and acknowledgement
practices, handling conflicts of interest, understanding the peer review process, how to handle reviewers' comments
and feedback, revising the manuscript and improving clarity, preparing for resubmission and managing rejection,
journal targeting and submission strategies.
Weeks 8- 11: Practical class on paper development � Weekly practical sessions for paper development and faculty
guidance for final manuscript preparation. By the end of week 11 students will share the draft of the paper with their
peers.
Week 12: Paper development workshop: peer feedback on paper drafts, one-on-one professors feedback, finalizing and
polishing the paper for submission.
Type of assessment:
Literature review assignment - 20%
Research proposal and contribution statement - 20% Methods and
results section draft - 20%
Peer review of a classmate�s draft - 10% Final
paper submission - 30%

Name: Introduction to Machine Learning Applications for Sustainability Research
Professors:
Roberta de Castro Souza Piao
Tiago Fonseca Albuquerque Cavalcanti Sigahi
Objectives:
To introduce students to the fundamentals of supervised and unsupervised machine learning, with a
focus on practical applications in contexts related to sustainability.
Rationale:
This is an introductory course aimed at providing a solid foundation for understanding key concepts,
techniques, and workflows in machine learning, using Python and modern libraries for data analysis,
visualization, and predictive modeling. The course will explore examples and case studies connected to
sustainability challenges such as climate change, circular economy, efficient resource use, sustainable
management, and responsible supply chains. By the end of the course, students are expected to be
able to perform basic data analyses and apply simple machine learning models for exploratory and
investigative purposes in research focused on sustainability.
Content:
Introductory concepts of supervised and unsupervised machine learning; types of machine learning
problems (classification/regression) and applications in operations management and sustainability
research; exploratory data analysis and data visualization with Python; data preprocessing techniques;
linear and logistic regression; instance-based methods: k-nearest neighbors (KNN); probabilistic
classifiers: Naive Bayes; maximum margin models: support vector machines (SVM); decision trees;
ensemble models: random forests; evaluation of predictive models; cross-validation and
hyperparameter tuning using grid search; clustering techniques: k-means; introductory concepts of
natural language processing (NLP) and sentiment analysis; hands-on implementation using Python
libraries (scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn, plotly, pandas, TextBlob); overview of sustainability research:
leading journals, research areas, key concepts, and examples; development of a final project applying
models to real-world problems in operations management and sustainability.

Name: Introduction to Linear Systems Theory
Professors: Diego Col�n
Objectives: Provide a theoretical foundation on continuous and discrete-time linear systems and state-space forms.
Rationale: Linear systems theory forms the basis of most control and signal processing methods.
Content:
 Notation and Mathematical Review: Matrices and Differential Equations.
 State Equations: Linearization and Implementation.
 Solution of State Equations: Existence, Uniqueness, Complete Solution.
 State Transition Matrix: General Properties.
 State Transition Matrix: Particular Cases.
 Internal Stability.
 Stability According to Lyapunov.
 (Particular Cases of Stability.)
 Controllability and Observability.
 Realization of Transfer Functions.
 Minimal Realizations.
 (Input-Output Stability.)
 Controllable and Observable Forms.
 Linear Feedback: Stabilization and Eigenvalue Allocation.
 State Observation: Observers and Stabilization by Output Feedback.
 (Special topics.)
Items in parentheses may be covered depending on the class interest and course progress.
The others represent the core of the course.
Type of assessment: Homework and final exam.

Name: Scientific Writing
Professors:
F�bio Lu�s Forti
Deborah Schechtman
Objectives:
Give students the experience in organizing data and preparing scientific texts for publication in
indexed international scientific journals. Increase the international visibility of the scientific
achievements of the students in the Biochemistry graduate program.
Rationale:
Preparing scientific manuscripts in the proper form for peer review and publication in high quality
journals is an essential quality for a successful scientific career. It is important that each student in our graduate program is independently capable of preparing a complete scientific manuscript. This course will require all students in the graduate program in biochemistry to organize their data and present it in the form of a complete scientific manuscript, according to international standards.
Content:
The course has three lectures. The course begins with an introductory lecture �Analysis of a
scientific paper�, which is followed by a scientific writing lecture and lecture on how to interact with
editors and reviewers during the peer review process. In addition to these lectures, the students
enrolled in the course will independently organize data obtained in graduate school in the form of a
complete manuscript for submission to an international scientific journal Qualis A of his choice. The
manuscript should follow the format and submission guidelines of the chosen journal. The
professors will schedule a date for the students to present their work in progress (usually a layout
of the manuscript) where they will receive specific feedback. The finished manuscript should be
submitted in PDF format accompanied by a cover letter using the discipline Moodle platform. The
professors will then act as editors, sending the manuscripts for peer review to professors
accredited in the graduate program and/or fellows in the biochemistry postdoctoral program. The
reviewers will critically and anonymously evaluate the manuscripts submitted considering both the
scientific content, as well as writing and presentation and suggest changes as they see fit. The
professors will mediate the correspondence between reviewers and students as part of their
editorial role. The students will be required to respond to the reviewers� comments and suggestions
and to submit a revised version of the manuscript. The students will be evaluated by the professors
according to the original manuscript submitted, reviewer comments on the manuscript, response to
reviewers and revised manuscript. Students that can attest first authorship in published or
accepted manuscripts to Qualis A scientific journals related to their thesis work will be exempt from
the peer review process and will be evaluated directly by the professors in charge based on the
submitted materials related to the publication (article, cover letter, reviewers� comments, and
response to reviewers).
Type of assessment:
course should be taken after the qualifying exam, thus ensuring that the students will have
accumulated enough experimental data to prepare a scientific manuscript.
Bibliography:
V.E. McMillan (2006), Writing Papers in the Biological Sciences. Bedford/St. Martin's Press. R.A.
Day, B. Gastel (2006) How to Write and Publish a Scientific Paper: 6th Edition. Greenwood Press.
M. Davis (2004) Scientific Papers and Presentations, Second Edition. Academic Press.
