Name: Computational methods for viromics research
Professors: Ant�nio Pedro de Castello Branco da Rocha Camargo
Objectives:
This hands-on course provides graduate students with a comprehensive foundation for
conducting computational research in viromics, the study of viruses in natural
ecosystems through high-throughput computational analysis. Students will develop a
deep understanding of the core theoretical concepts underlying the field alongside
practical experience with the computational tools used in modern research. Through
guided analysis of real datasets, students will build a toolkit for working with
metagenomes, viral genomes, and viral proteins, and learn the best practices that ensure
reliable data analysis. The course places a strong emphasis on understanding the
underlying principles underlying computational methods, enabling students to make
informed decisions and draw reliable biological insights from data.
Rationale:
The exponential growth of metagenomic sequencing data has provided unprecedented
power to characterize the diversity and ecology of microbiomes across ecosystems
worldwide. As microbiome research has gained increasing attention in environments
ranging from oceans and soils to the human body and industrial bioreactors, so too has
the study of the viruses infecting these microbial communities. In recent years, our
knowledge of viral diversity and biology has expanded substantially, revealing entirely
new types of viruses that infect a broad range of cellular organisms. Navigating and
contributing to this fast-evolving field requires proficiency in best practices for analyzing
metagenomes, viral genomes, and viral proteins. This course equips graduate students
with the computational and conceptual tools to independently analyze large-scale
sequencing datasets and conduct rigorous, cutting-edge research in viromics and viral
ecology.
Content:
Introduction to metagenomics, viromics, and viral ecology
Viral genome diversity and genome architecture
Discovery of uncultivated viral genomes from sequencing data
Assessing viral genome quality
Taxonomic classification of viral genomes
Predicting hosts of uncultivated viruses
Predicting and characterizing viral protein sequences and structures
Databases and reference resources for computational research in viromics
Type of assessment:
Students will complete a small research project developed throughout the course. They
will work in groups, with each group selecting a unique topic. Final grades will be based
on each group�s presentation on the last day of the course.
The final grade will be assigned according to the following scale:
- 8.5 to 10.0: A
- 7.0 to 8.4: B
- 5.0 to 6.9: C
- 0.0 to 4.9: R
Bibliography:
Roux, Simon, and Clement Coclet. "Viromics approaches for the study of viral diversity
and ecology in microbiomes." Nature Reviews Genetics 27.1 (2026): 32-46.

Name: Systems Thinking for Management and Organizations
Professors:
Lara Bartocci Liboni
Luciana Oranges Cezarino
Objectives:
1.	Recognize when and how to frame complex problems in a systems approach.
2.	Unlearn some of the deeply ingrained thought-patterns that result in linear thinking or unintentional biases.
3.	Understand the basic tenets of systems thinking (e.g., feedback loops, flows and limits, delays, leverage points, etc.) � knowledge, comprehension � content-related outcomes.
4.	Through modelling and analysis of 'leverage points,' identify more effective managerial responses.
5.	Learn how to build system capacity through collaborative governance; working together to generate impact and adaptations across businesses and systems.
6.	Become adept at identifying how systems and critical thinking can be used in a business context.
Rationale:
Managerial action is often conceived as a linear process of identifying and solving problems. This approach tends to isolate parts of organizational reality, as if problems could be separated from the contexts in which they emerge and solved through technical, predictable, and standardized responses. However, contemporary organizations operate in environments shaped by interdependence, uncertainty, social tensions, digital transformation, climate change, legitimacy crises, new forms of work, and increasing demands for sustainability.
In this context, it is necessary to educate managers who are able to understand complex situations as interconnected systems, in which local decisions may generate broad, indirect, and often unintended consequences. Systems thinking provides conceptual and practical tools to diagnose organizational and socio-environmental problems in an integrated way, considering the relationships among people, technologies, structures, routines, strategies, institutions, and the natural environment.
The course is justified by its contribution to a more reflective, critical, and strategic managerial education. Rather than simply applying linear models of planning and control, students will be encouraged to map interdependencies, recognize multiple perspectives, identify leverage points, and propose interventions that are more consistent with the complexity of organizations and their ecosystems. The course engages with contemporary issues in management, such as innovation, artificial intelligence, digital platforms, sustainability, biodiversity, supply chains, governance, organizational change, and new configurations of work.
Content:
1.	Introduction to systems thinking, linear thinking, and complexity 
2.	Epistemology of systems thinking 
3.	Core principles of systems: wholeness, interdependence, feedback, and emergence 
4.	Systemic intervention methodologies 
5.	System dynamics 
6.	Organizational learning and systemic change 
7.	Systems mapping, analysis, and diagnosis: structures and behaviors 
8.	Applying systems thinking to managerial problems and complex solutions 
9.	Synthesis: looking back, looking forward
Type of assessment: In accordance with Article 66 of the USP Post-Graduation Regulations, students must achieve a minimum attendance rate of 75%, which does not influence the final score. Performance is assessed on a scale from 0 to 10 converted into the following grades: A (9.0�10.0), B (7.5�8.9), C (6.0�7.4), and R (below 6.0). The final score is based on class participation (20%), seminars, exercises, or case studies (30%), and an individual and/or group final project (50%). Any other assessment guidelines will be provided at the beginning of the course.

Name: Multilevel Modelling
Professors: Perla Calil Pongeluppe Wadhy Rebehy
Objectives:
By the end of the course, participants will be able to:
1. Recognise hierarchical data structures and justify the use of MLM over traditional OLS
regression.
2. Specify, estimate, interpret, and compare linear and nonlinear multilevel models.
3. Incorporate cross-level interactions, centring strategies, and model diagnostics.
4. Implement MLM in R (primary) and understand implementations in Stata and Mplus.
5. Critically evaluate model assumptions, convergence issues, power, and interpretability.
6. Apply advanced topics (Bayesian MLM, longitudinal models, meta-analysis) and discuss
future directions.
7. Design, execute, and present a complete MLM analysis relevant to their own PhD research
Rationale:
The increasing generation and structuring of information on social networks and in public and
private institutions has demanded the training of human resources capable of processing and
analyzing this data. Therefore, this course provides training in statistical techniques for
interpreting big data, as well as the analytical capacity to formulate questions based on the
available universe of information.
Content:
1. Introduction to MLM: definition, hierarchical data structures
2. Foundations: fixed vs random effects, ICC, Level-1/Level-2 predictors, core
assumptions
3. Linear MLM: model specification (random intercept, random slope), estimation
methods (ML vs REML)
4. Building models: centring (group-mean vs grand-mean), cross-level interactions, model
comparison (LRT, AIC, BIC), handling missing data
5. Model fit & diagnostics: deviance, AIC/BIC, residual analysis
6. Nonlinear MLM: generalised linear mixed models
7. Advanced topics (Part 1): multilevel SEM, Bayesian MLM, longitudinal/growth-curve
models
8. Advanced topics (Part 2) + software case studies: multilevel meta-analysis
9. Challenges and limitations: convergence issues, sample size/power, assumption
violations, interpretability of complex models
Type of assessment:
The assessment strategies used are based on the chosen teaching-learning strategies:
� Development of a multilevel model (which can be applied to the graduate student's
thesis or dissertation) based on an article published in Q1 (Scopus) (50%);
� Case study with presentation based on the Model developed (50%).
Bibliography:
1. Full book: Multilevel Modelling by Miguel Matos Torres (2026) � required reading.
2. Lecture slides and complete R scripts (supplied for each session)
3. Public and book-specific datasets (educational achievement, health outcomes,
organisational behaviour, etc.)
4. �MLM Cheat-Sheet� with common commands

Name: Seminars in Cellular and Molecular Biology I
Professors:
Luis Lamberti Pinto da Silva
Dario Sim�es Zamboni
Mariana Kiomy Osako
Objectives: Create a forum for debate and constant updating on themes and research carried out in Cellular and Molecular Biology. Students should get in touch with the frontiers of knowledge in these areas and are expected to develop critical behavior and complement individual training in what is not acquired through thesis work and other disciplines of a specific nature.
Rationale: Monitoring conceptual and methodological advances is essential for the training of graduate students in Cellular and Molecular Biology
Content: The discipline will be taught in the form of seminars on subjects of impact and high relevance in Cellular or Molecular Biology. They will be given by doctoral students of the Program and by invited researchers, from Brazil or abroad. As the frontier of knowledge always presents high mobility and some uncertainty, it may happen that a theme presented in one year is discussed again, with the inclusion of new material in the following year, reflecting the conceptual advance observed in the period. In this way, the listing of the same topics in the four disciplines (RBP-5766, 5767, 5768 and 5769) only indicates the areas that we currently see as relevant. We can highlight: Regulation of Gene Expression, Genomics and Functional Genomics, Signal Transduction, Intercellular Communication, Cytoskeleton, Molecular Developmental Biology, Bioinformatics, Molecular and cellular aspects of infections
Type of assessment: Student performance in the presentation and content of abstracts on the subject matter. These summaries will be delivered by students at the end of each presentation.
Bibliography: Updated every six months, based on the topics covered. The journals with the greatest impact in the area (Science, Cell, Nature, Current Opinion, Embo Journal, Molecular and Cell Biology) and the current year will be used as a bibliographic source
Selection criteria: Contato: lldasilva@fmrp.usp.br

Name: Advanced Aspects of Gene Expression Regulation
Professors:
Rinaldo Flores Catta Preta
Tathiane Maistro Malta Pereira
Objectives: This course provides a comprehensive exploration of computational biology and genomics, covering both foundational aspects of the regulation of gene expression and advanced analytical techniques. Students will engage in conceptual discussions, as well as working demos with some state-of-the-art data analysis methods.
Rationale: While models of gene expression have been established, its regulation remains an area of intense research, due to its complexity and high dimensionality. Several genomic features and gene products have been associated with the mechanisms by which cells modulate the transcription of their genes and synthesis of gene end products, i.e. proteins and ncRNA. Gene products participate in gene expression regulation as transcription factors, co-activators and repressors of transcriptional machinery, chromatin state and conformation modulators, modulation of post-transcriptional processes, besides transducing intrinsic and extrinsic signals. In eukaryotes, these signals are critical in development, but also to maintain tissue homeostasis; failure to maintain system health contribute to several diseases. Most studies aimed at unveiling the complex regulation of gene expression in mammals are conducted in model organisms, largely isogenic, making translation to human health and disease more challenging. Human genomes carry many variants that are frequently ancestry-related, some of which have been implicated in serious health conditions, while many of them contribute to complex disease risk. This course will include an overview of the current understanding of gene expression regulation and some of the methods used to probe it at bulk and single-cell level, focusing on their limitations and considerations for the data interpretation. Also included is how this foundational knowledge can be used to interrogate genetic variation, and its consequence for dysregulation in disease.
Content:
1.	Regulation of gene expression
�	Transcription
�	Post-transcription
�	Protein translation
�	Post-translational processes

2.	Chromatin Conformation
�	Tridimensional chromatin
�	Biological condensates

3.	Genetic variation
�	Genome Wide Association Studies (GWAS)
�	Implication in system homeostasis

4.	Modern statistics in (epi)genomic data analysis
�	Non-parametric data analysis

5.	Assay types
�	RNA-seq*, bulk and single-cell
�	ChIP-seq*, Cut&Run
�	ATAC-seq*
�	Hi-C

6.	Integrative data analysis
�	Trends
�	Deterministic and probabilistic approaches
�	Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence

* Besides methodological presentation, students will receive data analysis tutorials for the techniques marked with (*) and will have the opportunity to practice with provided examples or own data, compatible with the sessions� durations.
Type of assessment: The evaluation will be based on interactive exercises completed during the theoretical presentation (50% weight), practical computational exercises performed by students (25% weight), and the final course report (25% weight). To pass, students must obtain a minimum final grade of C. Students achieving more than 90% will receive an A, those between 80% and 90% will receive a B, those between 70% and 80% will receive a C, and those below 70% will receive an R. Grades will be statistically graded.

Objectives: Promote the interaction of graduate students in Biochemistry with lines of research developed on the campus of Ribeir�o Preto, the country, and eventually in laboratories abroad.

Name: Topics in Contemporary Biochemistry II
Professors:
Tie Koide
Roberto do Nascimento Silva
Objectives: To create a forum for debates on recent and general advances in Biochemistry in order to allow students to acquire up-to-date knowledge and develop critical behavior, complementing individual training that cannot be acquired through thesis work and other specific disciplines.
Rationale: The follow-up of conceptual and methodological advances is fundamental for the training of doctoral students in the field of Biochemistry. The subjects presented in this course will serve as the basis for the doctoral qualification examination in Biochemistry.
Content:
The theoretical program of this discipline will consist of updated reviews on subjects that have a broad interest in biochemistry and that will constitute the subject matter discussed in the seminars. 
The Seminar program will be based on scientific works that are characterized as a frontier of knowledge in a particular area of Biochemistry, emphasizing the following subjects: 
1.	Hormones, Messengers and Growth Factors. 
2.	Molecular Aspects of Development, Immunology, Evolution, Medicine, Parasites and Microorganisms. 
3.	Recombinant DNA. 
4.	Regulation of gene expression. 
5.	Structural Biology (higher levels of organization). 
6.	Ubiquitination and intracellular proteolysis. 
7.	Organization of Membranes, etc. 

As the frontier of knowledge always presents high mobility and some uncertainty, it may occur that a topic presented in a year (eg: RBQ-5741) is discussed again in this discipline, with the inclusion of new material, reflecting the conceptual advance observed in the period. Thus, the listing of the same subjects in the two courses (RBQ-5741, RBQ-5742) indicates the current areas of knowledge that the Program wishes to cover. It will be developed 6-7 themes per year.
Type of assessment: It will be done depending on the student's performance in the presentation and participation in the Seminars.
Bibliography: Bibliography is updated every semester, based on subjects covered in the Annual Reviews and other reviews of Biochemistry, Genetics, Molecular Biology, etc.
Selection criteria: Contato: tkoide@fmrp.usp.br

Name: Biochemical Roles of Extracellular Vesicles Involved in the Bone Mineralization
Professors:
Pietro Ciancaglini
Mayt� Bolean Correia
Massimo Bottini
Objectives: The objective of this course is to describe cellular vesicles found in tissues that undergo the biomineralization process. Special emphasis will be given to the description of the vesicles' composition, biochemical and biophysical properties, physiological and pathological roles, and isolation and characterization methods. The biophysical methods used to evaluate the interactions between vesicles and important macromolecules, such as collagen, will also be described.
Rationale: The current (bio)chemistry course does not cover the most recent advances in evaluating the biochemical and biophysical aspects of cellular vesicles and their biochemical and biophysical properties
Content:
1.	Introduction: intracellular and extracellular vesicles.
2.	Extracellular vesicles: Biogenesis, physiological and pathological roles.
3.	Biochemical and biophysical properties of biomineralizing vesicles: proteomics and lipidomics.
4.	Vesicle isolation methods.
5.	Vesicle characterization methods.
6.	Interactions of mineralizing vesicles with macromolecules (e.g., collagen fibers).
Type of assessment: Critical analysis of an article chosen by the teacher

Name: Academic writing for business research
Professors:
Andr� Feliciano Lino
Ricardo Rocha de Azevedo
Rationale: As research in the applied social sciences is communicated through texts, these texts must be read and understood by an audience to have relevance and meaning. This course aims to highlight the key aspects that researchers can train in to develop their academic writing skills. In short, academic writing is a fundamental skill for researchers to enter and remain active in the scientific field. More than mastering theoretical or methodological content, it is essential to learn how to communicate findings in a persuasive, clear, and journal-appropriate manner. This communication takes place within an institutional context that involves editorial practices, peer review, and formal and informal rules that shape what is considered publishable. Furthermore, mastering academic writing enables researchers not only to disseminate results but also to actively participate in scholarly conversations, contributing to the advancement of knowledge and the consolidation of an academic and research career.
Content:
1. The Institutional Environment and Standards of Academic Writing in Business 
The first session introduces participants to the institutional context of academic production in the applied social sciences, particularly in the field of business. It covers the writing standards required by academic journals, the pressures of productivity, the role of writing as a central tool in scientific activity, and the effects of this environment on the quality and relevance of the knowledge produced. Emphasis is placed on the importance of writing that is not only technical but also persuasive and accessible to diverse audiences.

2. The Research Problem and the Significance of the Study
The second session explores how to construct a relevant research problem and articulate it convincingly. Strategies are presented to highlight the practical and theoretical significance of the phenomenon under investigation. The session emphasizes the importance of engaging the reader from the very beginning, connecting them with real-world issues and the dilemmas of the research field.

3. Theorized Storyline: Problematizing the Literature, Constructing the Gap, or Solving a Real-World Problem
This session focuses on structuring a 'theorized storyline,' a concept based on Golden-Biddle and Locke. The aim is to provide an argumentative roadmap that articulates the significance of the phenomenon, situates the study within the existing literature, problematizes that literature strategically (through incomplete, inconsistent, or inadequate gaps), and offers an original contribution.

4. Situated Methodology: Going Beyond Describing Methods
The fourth session treats methodology as a crucial element for building the study�s credibility. It argues that methods should not merely be described but situated�that is, justified in light of the research context and objectives. Topics include the researcher�s competence, integrity, and benevolence, as well as the importance of positionality, particularly in qualitative research. Cases involving sensitive data collection and field access strategies are also discussed.

5. Presenting Data and Analysis
In this session, participants learn how to structure the results section persuasively, balancing empirical data presentation with theoretical interpretation. Especially for qualitative research, narrative strategies such as the Setup�Quotation�Commentary (SQC) model, the use of direct quotes versus paraphrasing, and the importance of both 'showing' and 'telling' with rhetorical intention are discussed. The integration of data and theory is highlighted as the climax of the scientific narrative.

6. Acting as a Reviewer, Peer Review, and Its Effects on Academic Texts
The final session addresses the peer review process as an integral part of academic narrative construction. It discusses the role of reviewers as silent co-authors and the impact of their suggestions on the structure of the text. Practical tips are offered on how to receive, interpret, and respond to reviews, as well as on sharing drafts through informal networks to gather qualified feedback. The session also underscores the importance of continuous revision and rewriting as part of the maturation of academic writing.
Type of assessment:
Participation and engagement in class discussions (30%)
Development of a draft introduction for an article/thesis/dissertation, including peer feedback (70%)

Name: Advanced Topics in Neurology - Mitochondrial Diseases
Professors: Claudia Ferreira da Rosa Sobreira
Rationale:
Since the 1980s, there has been great advance in the knowledge of mitochondrial diseases and their
pathophysiological basis. In particular, the study of mitochondrial DNA allowed the elucidation of previously
unknown aspects of mitochondrial biogenesis. In recent years, the association of nuclear genes (encoded
by nuclear DNA) with diseases caused by mitochondrial dysfunction and, in some cases, related to
secondary alterations of mitochondrial DNA, has opened new frontiers on the knowledge of the various
functions if this organelle. The in-depth study of the pathophysiology of mitochondrial diseases is important
not only for increasing the effectiveness of diagnosis and the understanding of the disease, but also for

contributing to the elucidation of unknown aspects of mitochondrial biogenesis and function. Furthermore,
the study of mitochondrial genetics and experimental models of mitochondrial diseases are crucial for the
development of new treatments, such as gene therapy. The opportunity to discuss the pathophysiological
mechanisms, the molecular abnormalities and the experimental models of the mitochondrial diseases with
postgraduate students may increase their interest in researches on the field.

Name: Non-invasive Transcranial Neuromodulation I
Professors:
Jo�o Pereira Leite
Taiza Elaine Grespan Santos Edwards
Oct�vio Marques Pontes Neto
Objectives:
�	To promote basic knowledge about physics, physiological and safety concepts of non-invasive transcranial neuromodulation using electrical and magnetic stimulation;
�	To develop critical analysis of clinical trials designs in non-invasive transcranial neuromodulation applied to patients with cerebrovascular diseases.
Rationale: Non-invasive transcranial neuromodulation has been reported as a potential treatment to promote neuroplasticity and functional recovery. The physics, physiological and safety aspects of non-invasive transcranial neuromodulation are essential to clinical research. Moreover, the development of critical analysis of clinical trial designs on neuromodulation is necessary for the learning program of post-graduate students.
Content:
�	Principles of non-invasive transcranial neuromodulation: Electrical stimulation
�	Safety aspects of transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS)
�	Principles of neuromodulation non-invasive transcranial: Magnetic stimulation
�	Safety aspects of transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS)
�	Computational models of tDCS 
�	Non-invasive transcranial neuromodulation and Neuroplasticity
�	Non-invasive transcranial neuromodulation and Neurorehabilitation in patients with cerebrovascular diseases
�	Clinical trials in tDCS
�	Clinical trials in TMS
Type of assessment:
In the first day of the course, students will be divided in 3 groups and the themes of each group will be selected. Each group will present one paper that will be used as part of the evaluation of the course. Moreover, each student will be responsible for the analysis of one scientific paper related to the theme of his/her group and should do a report following the instructions of a letter to editor of the respective journal. 
�	Group presentation of the paper: 30% of the grade.
�	Letter to editor: 70% of the grade.

Name: Quality Improvement Project: Science Applied to Practice
Professors: Walusa Assad Gon�alves Ferri
Objectives:
Course Description
Theoretical and practical foundations of quality improvement (QI) methodology in healthcare. Structured approach for developing projects applied to clinical or organizational practice, focusing on patient safety, effectiveness, and care experience. Problem analysis tools, indicator development, and impact evaluation. Emphasis on projects targeting child and adolescent health.
________________________________________
Course Objectives
�	General Objective:
To train students to develop and implement quality improvement projects (QIPs), based on scientific evidence, in services focused on child and adolescent health.
�	Specific Objectives:
1.	Understand the core concepts of quality improvement.
2.	Identify problems and improvement opportunities in pediatric settings.
3.	Apply tools for analyzing, planning, executing, and evaluating improvement projects, such as PDSA cycles, root cause analysis, flowcharts, and indicators.
4.	Apply quality improvement tools.
5.	Foster the integration of science with clinical practice in care environments.
6.	Use data collection and analysis methods to monitor and evaluate QIPs.
7.	Integrate concepts of patient safety, teamwork, and organizational culture into projects.
8.	Promote the connection between theory and practice through a project developed in the student's professional context.
9.	Develop a real and contextualized improvement project with measurable impact.
Rationale:
Justification
The increasing complexity of healthcare systems requires professional performance based not only on technical-scientific knowledge but also on systematic strategies for continuous quality improvement. The implementation of QIPs has proven essential in promoting patient safety, service efficiency, and user satisfaction. This course aims to equip students with the skills to develop and apply evidence-based improvement projects with direct impact on clinical and organizational practice, reinforcing a commitment to excellence in healthcare
Content:
Syllabus / Program Content
Class 1: Foundations of Quality Improvement in Healthcare
�	History and evolution of QIPs
�	Concepts of quality and patient safety
Class 2: Problem Identification and Opportunities for Improvement
�	Methods for identifying failures
�	Care priorities
Class 3: Quality Improvement Tools I
�	Ishikawa Diagram, 5 Whys, Structured Brainstorming
Class 4: Quality Improvement Tools II
�	PDSA cycle, flowchart, prioritization matrix
Class 5: Data Collection and Analysis in QIPs
�	Quality indicators
�	Basic tools for applied statistics
Class 6: Project Development (Part 1)
�	Problem definition, SMART goals, team, schedule
Class 7: Project Development (Part 2)
�	Action plan, process and outcome indicators, evaluation plan
Class 8: Safety Culture and Leadership in Improvement Projects
�	Team engagement, resistance to change, effective communication
Class 9: Practical Applications and Case Studies
�	Presentation and discussion of real projects
Class 10: Group Practice � Building an Applied QIP
�	Workshop with faculty supervision
Classes 11 & 12: Final Project Presentations
�	Evaluation and structured feedback from faculty
________________________________________
Program Content Table
Class	Topic	Activities
1	Foundations of Quality Improvement	Lecture and guided discussion
2	Problem Identification	Case study and prioritization of problem
3	Quality Tools I	Workshop with Ishikawa Diagram, 5 Whys
4	Quality Tools II	Workshop with PDSA, flowcharts, decision matrix
5	Indicator Development	Practical class with real examples
6	Data Collection and Analysis	Introduction to applied statistics
7	Project Development � Part 1	Define problem, SMART goals, team, and plan
8	Project Development � Part 2	Indicators, implementation cycle, and evaluation
9	Patient Safety and Organizational Culture	Seminar and debate
10	Case Studies in Pediatrics	Analysis of QIPs in pediatric hospitals
11	Project Presentations � Part 1	Oral presentation with feedback
12	Project Presentations � Part 2	Oral presentation with feedback
________________________________________

Methodology
�	Interactive lectures
�	Case studies and practical workshops
�	Group work with faculty mentorship
�	Applied project presentation
Type of assessment:
Assessment:
1. Participation in theoretical and practical activities (weight 2)
2. Development and presentation of the applied QIP (weight 6)
3. Participation in seminars to discuss cases and tools (weight 2)


Evaluation
Criterion	Weight
Participation in theoretical and practical activities	20%
Seminar and workshop presentations	20%
Final Project (Applied QIP)	60%

Name: Brain-Computer Interfaces
Professors: Jo�o Luis Garcia Rosa
Objectives:
To introduce the basic concepts of Brain-Computer Interfaces.
To analyze the behavior of these models, their fundamental capabilities and
limitations.
Rationale:
Several computing techniques have been proposed inspired by neuroscience. These techniques have
been successfully applied to solve a large number of practical problems. This success has encouraged
the theoretical and practical development of this field of computational neuroscience.
Content:
Basic neuroscience: Neurons, Dendrites and axons, Synapses, Neuron populations, Hodgkin-Huxley
Model, Action potential, Synaptic plasticity. Deterministic neurodynamics: State space, Stability,
Attractors. Neuron populations: Freeman's K-sets. Recording and stimulating brain activity: Recording
the brain, Invasive techniques, Non-invasive techniques; stimulating the brain: Invasive and non-invasive
techniques; Recording and stimulation; EEG: History, Recordings, Artifacts, EP, ERP, Analysis. Signal
processing: Frequency domain analysis, Wavelet analysis, Time domain analysis, Spatial filtering,
Artifact reduction techniques. Machine Learning for BCI: Classification and Regression Techniques,
Applications. Types of BCI: Invasive, Semi-invasive, non-invasive. Applications and ethics.
Type of assessment:
3 seminars: S1, S2 and S3. 
AS = Seminar Grade Average 
Final grade
If (8.5 ≤ AS ≤ 10.0): final grade A 
If (7.0 ≤ AS < 8.5): final grade B 
If (5.0 ≤ AS < 7.0): final grade C
Bibliography:
Basic readings 
W. J. Freeman and R. Quian-Quiroga, Imaging Brain Function With EEG - Advanced Temporal and Spatial
Analysis of Electroencephalographic Signals. Springer, 2013.
R. P. N. Rao, Brain-Computer Interfacing - An Introduction, Cambridge University Press, 2013.

Additional readings
S. Haykin, Neural Networks and Learning Machines. 3rd. Ed. Pearson, 2009.
E. M. Izhikevich, Dynamical Systems in Neuroscience: The Geometry of Excitability and Bursting, The MIT
Press, 2007.

2000.
M. A. Arbib (Ed.), The Handbook of Brain Theory and Neural Networks, 2nd. edition, A Bradford Book. The
MIT Press, 2003.

Name: Introduction to Semantic Web
Professors: Dilvan de Abreu Moreira
Objectives: To introduce students to techniques, concepts and models used in the Semantic Web field, enabling them to develop resources, tools and computational applications to solve problems in the field of Web applications. To enable students to critically discuss subjects related to the field and to support research development.
Rationale: The Semantic Web is an extension of the current Web, with the purpose to assign meaning to the published content on the Internet, so that it may be read and properly handled by both humans and computers. One of its goals is the development of a technological model that allows machine-assisted global sharing of knowledge. Several computer science fields have adopted Semantic Web technologies and standards (from the W3C) for the automatic exchange of knowledge among applications, databases and knowledge bases, in areas such as biomedical computing, web systems, and ubiquitous computing, among others. For this reason, it is important to provide to interested students an overview of the Semantic Web field, its problems, and the solutions it promises for the current Web.
Content: 1- Semantic Web. 2- RDF and RDFS. 3- Linked Data: concepts and SPARQL. 4- Web Ontology Language (OWL) and Description Logic. 5- OWL Profiles: OWL-EL, OWL-QL and OWL-RL. 6- Applications: Creating na ontology and using classifiers (suggestion: Prot�g�). 7- Semantic Web Rule Language (SWRL).
Type of assessment: Tests and practical projects.
Bibliography:
Main texts:
Semantic Web for the working ontologist : effective modeling in RDFS and OWL / Dean Allemang, Jim Hendler. � 2nd ed. 2011 Elsevier Inc., ISBN 978-0-12-385965-5
Antoniou, G. e Harmelen, F. 2008. A Semantic Web Primer. Second edition. The MIT Press. London. ISBN: 978-0262012423.
Bob DuCharm, Learning SPARQL, O'Reilly 2011, ISBN 978-1-449-30659-5.
Baader, Franz, Diego Calvanese, Deborah McGuinness, Daniele Nardi, and Peter Patel-Schneider, eds. The Description Logic Handbook: Theory, Implementation and Applications. Cambridge University Press, 2003, ISBN 0521781760.
Breitman, K. K. ; Casanova, M. A.; Truszkowski, Walt .(2007) Semantic Web: Concepts, Technologies and Applications - NASA Monographs in Systems and Software Engineering Series. 1. ed. London: Springer Verlag London, 329 p.

Additional texts:
Journal and conference papers in the field.

Ana Am�lia Benedito Silva
Camilo Rodrigues Neto

Carlos Molina Mendes
Objectives: This course aims to introduce the elements of complex system modeling through many examples, which also illustrate the basic tools to face them.
Rationale: The students of the graduate program in Complex Systems Modeling have different backgrounds. Therefore, there is a need for a course that presents some basic tools and languages that enable them to develop their projects.
Content:
1- Differential equations
1.1) Brief revision of differential and integral calculus (of a single and several variables)
1.2) Elements of ordinary and partial differential equations (population growth models,
interpretation of the master equation, etc)
2- Linear algebra
2.1) Matrices and linear systems
2.2) Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
3- Probability theory
3.1) Random variables
3.2) Probability distributions
3.3) Expectation
4- Introduction to programming
4.1) Monte Carlo methods
4.2) Numerical calculus
Type of assessment:
The grading is based on problem sets (which encompasses exercises on programming and calculus / linear algebra and are individual tasks): a final problem set (grade Ef, with 0 <= Ef <= 10.0) and a set of weekly minor problem sets (about fifteen) with equal weights (the average grade Em of these minor problem sets
obeys 0.0 <= Em <= 10.0). Let M be the minimum between Em and Ef. The attribution of the final grade is given by:
(i) 0.0 <= M < 5.0: R (course failure)
(ii) 5.0 <= M < 7.0: C
(iii) 7.0 <= M < 9.0: B
(iv) 9.0 <= M <= 10.0: A

The course frequency is not part of the grading crit

Name: Simulation of Complex Systems I
Professors:
Camilo Rodrigues Neto
Jos� Ricardo Gon�alves de Mendon�a
Marcelo de Souza Lauretto
Carlos Molina Mendes
Diego Antonio Falceta Gon�alves
Alexandre Ferreira Ramos
Objectives:
The goal of the discipline is to present basic techniques of statistical
simulations and complex systems. The student should understand the main ideas and
justifications of the concept of simulation and be able to perform their own simulations of
simple models of interest in the area.
Rationale:
Complex systems can hardly be treated analytically except in the
simplest cases. The modeling of complex systems, therefore, depends heavily on
computational studies, in particular on statistical simulations (Monte Carlo and Markov chain
simulations and the analysis of large volumes of data, experimental and simulational). Due to
the students� enormously varied training backgrounds when entering the graduate program in
Complex Systems Modeling, it is imperative that there be a discipline that addresses the main
theoretical and practical elements of statistical and computational modeling of complex
systems.
Content:
[1] Review of probability and statistics: (i) probability: sample
space, independent events, conditional probability, Bayes' theorem; (ii) random variables:
probability functions (density, cumulative), main discrete and continuous distributions,
bivariate, marginal and conditional distributions, iid samples, transformation of random
variables; (iii) expectation: properties, variance and covariance, conditional expectation,
generative function of moments; (iv) laws of large numbers and the central limit theorem; (v)
estimation and decision theory: parameter estimation, point estimators, least squares,
maximum likelihood and Bayesian estimation, hypothesis tests and decision tests.
Introduction to statistical simulation languages: useful commands, basic operations,
operators, data types, conversion of special types and values, import and export of data,
control functions, data segmentation, basic statistics, graphics, basic programming. [3]
Generation of random variables: uniform random variables and their uses, inverse
transformation, general methods of transformation of random variables, normal variables,
discrete distributions, acceptance-rejection methods. [4] Monte Carlo integration: classical
algorithm, importance sampling, resampling, selection of importance function. [5] Metropolis-
Hastings method: brief introduction to Markov chains, basic Metropolis-Hastings algorithm,
generic Monte Carlo algorithm by Markov chain, random walks, simulation of social
interaction models (Schelling, Axelrod and Sznajd models).
Type of assessment: see notes

Name: Estimation, Identification and Stochastic Filtering
Professors: Marcos Rogerio Fernandes
Objectives: The course aims to develop the fundamentals of estimation theory, system identification, and stochastic filtering for both linear and non-linear dynamic systems. Furthermore, applications across various fields will be presented. Special focus is placed on non-linear systems embedded in non-Gaussian noise and the use of Kalman Filter variations, such as the EKF (Extended Kalman Filter), UKF (Unscented Kalman Filter), and Particle Filters. Additionally, the course aims to incorporate new teaching practices intended to solidify knowledge of stochastic filtering theory for new generations of researchers, particularly in the field of dynamic systems.
Rationale: Given that state estimation and stochastic filtering tools are essential for various research fields related to Electrical Engineering such as system identification, time series forecasting, and control problems, this course seeks to provide a solid foundation in the fundamentals of estimation theory and stochastic filtering to serve students across diverse research areas within Electrical Engineering.
Content: 1) Review of Probability and Statistics; 2) Estimation Theory; 3) Identification of linear and non-linear stochastic dynamic systems; 4) Introduction to Bayesian Filtering and the Classical Kalman Filter; Extended Kalman Filter (EKF); Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF); Particle Filter (PF); 4) Performance analysis of stochastic filters; 5) Engineering applications such as inertial navigation, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and radar target tracking.
Type of assessment: The assessment will be carried out through Problem Sets (20%), a Final Project (30%), and a Theoretical Exam (50%). To pass, the student must obtain a final grade of 5.0 or higher.
Bibliography:
[1] S�rkk�, S.; Bayesian Filtering and Smoothing. Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 2013.
[2] Y. Bar Shalom, X. R. Li and T. Kirubarajan, �Estimation with Applications to Tracking and Navigation,� Wiley & Sons, New York, 2001.
[3] Ljung, L. System Identification: Theory for the User. 2nd Edition, Prentice Hall PTR, Upper Saddle River, 1999.
[4] Thrun, S., Burgard, W., Fox, D.; Probabilistic robotics. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, 2005.
[5] Challa S, Morelande MR, Mu�icki D, Evans RJ. Fundamentals of Object Tracking. Cambridge University Press; 2011.

Name: Modal Testing
Professors: Paulo Sergio Varoto
Objectives: To provide basic theoretical and experimental foundations in modal analysis and identification of mechanical systems, such that the student is capable of formulating mathematical models that describe the dynamic behavior of a given structure under study from data obtained through experimental tests as well as correlate these models with those obtained through computer simulations, for example, through the finite element method.
Rationale: Theoretical and experimental Modal Analysis is a powerful tool in the analysis of vibrations in mechanical systems. System identification techniques (in time and frequency domains) form the basis of modal analysis. The courses currently offered by our Post-Graduate Mechanical Engineering area in the area of structural dynamics and vibrations do not address these identification techniques and their use in research works becomes indispensable in the formulation of mathematical models that describe the dynamic behavior of machines. and systems. So, the Postgraduate area - AEM resents a course with the profile proposed here.
Content:
1. Introduction � General considerations on the identification of mechanical systems. 1.1 Philosophy of modal analysis of systems
1.2 Theoretical vs experimental modal analysis
1.3 Review of mechanical vibration concepts
1.3.1 Systems with one and two GDLs, modal superposition
1.3.2 Forced response of linear systems � FRF concept 1.3.3 MIMO systems � Input and output relationships
1.3.4 Acceleration transmissibility concepts
2. Measurement considerations
2.1 Description of the basic equipment used in modal analysis 2.2 Considerations on the binding of the structure under study
2.3 Types of excitation
2.4 Measurement of input and output signals
3. Systems identification techniques
3.1 Time domain identification methods
3.2 Frequency domain identification methods 3.3 Global identification methods
4. Correlation and adjustment of mathematical models
4.1 Sensitivity analysis of dynamic models
4.2 Mathematical model adjustment techniques
4.3 Correlation of experimental data with data obtained in computer simulations
Type of assessment: Weighted average grade between exams, projects and special problems. Relationship for conversion : R: [0-4], C: [5-6], B: [7-8], A:[9,10]

Name: Instability, transition and turbulence in fluids
Professors: Marcello Augusto Faraco de Medeiros
Objectives: Provide students with knowledge about the basic concepts of hydrodynamic  instability, transition to turbulence and turbulent flow.
Rationale: Most disciplines in fluid mechanics and aerodynamics deal mainly with  that have a transient regime. Among the transient flows, there is the turbulent flow which is the one observed in general in practical applications. The course will present an integrated view of transient phenomena in a sequence of increasing complexity. This sequence includes waves in fluids, instability waves in fluids, transition to turbulence and turbulent flows. In this way, the discipline presents knowledge in a more extensive and detailed way, always paying attention to a physical/phenomenological explanation of the problem, and presenting possible applications.
Type of assessment: Weighted average of assignments and assignment lists. Approval with an average greater than 50%

Name: Legged Robots
Professors: Thiago Boaventura Cunha
Objectives: Provide the graduate student with basic knowledge about legged robots, as biped and quadrupeds. The idea is that he/she can work in research centers, development companies, and robotics companies
Rationale:
Given the fast hardware and software development observed last years, legged robots are becoming a reality, both for indoor environments (e.g. factories, companies, offices, hospitals, etc.) as well as outdoor
environments (for instance, on the streets, in farms, etc.). In this context, a broader education is needed for graduate students. So, they can have a multidisciplinary knowledge that will allow them to work in research and development of solutions in this area.
Content:
Introduction and history of legged robots; Review of dynamic systems response; Fixed-base robot kinematics and dynamics review; Joint space and operating space; Force and position control; Basic
principles of physical interaction control; Mechanical impedance control; Stability and passivity of impedance controllers; Biomechanical principles of locomotion; Floating-base robot dynamics; Linear algebra review; Inverse dynamics of floating base robots; Balance and posture control of legged robots.
Type of assessment: MF=Average between exercises, homework, seminars, projects and tests. The rate conversion is as follows: A: [9-10], B: [7-8], C: [5-6], R: [0-4].

Name: Introduction to Machine Learning Applications for Sustainability Research
Professors: Lucas Gabriel Zanon
Objectives: This course aims to introduce students to the fundamentals of supervised and unsupervised machine learning, with a focus on practical applications in contexts related to sustainability. This is an introductory course aimed at providing a solid foundation for understanding key concepts, techniques, and workflows in machine learning, using Python and modern libraries for data analysis, visualization, and predictive modeling. The course will explore examples and case studies connected to sustainability challenges such as climate change, circular economy, efficient resource use, sustainable management, and responsible supply chains. By the end of the course, students are expected to be able to perform basic data analyses and apply simple machine learning models for exploratory and investigative purposes in research focused on sustainability.
Rationale: The growing availability of data and advances in machine learning techniques have created new opportunities for sustainability research. However, many researchers in production engineering and operations management still lack specific training in these techniques. This course is justified by the need to equip graduate students with modern computational tools for data analysis, enabling them to apply predictive and exploratory models to real-world sustainability problems. The course will be taught in English and offered jointly with Politecnico di Milano (Polimi) and Escola Polit�cnica da Universidade de S�o Paulo (Poli-USP), aiming at the internationalization of the program and academic exchange between the institutions.
Content:
1. Introductory concepts of supervised and unsupervised machine learning; 2. Types of machine learning problems (classification/regression) and applications in operations management and sustainability research; 3. Exploratory data analysis and data
visualization with Python; 4. Data preprocessing techniques; linear and logistic regression; 5. Instance-based methods: k-nearest neighbors (KNN); probabilistic classifiers: Naive Bayes; 6. Maximum margin models: support vector machines (SVM); decision trees; 7.
Ensemble models: random forests; 8. Evaluation of predictive models; 9. Cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning using grid search; 10. Clustering techniques: k-means; introductory concepts of natural language processing (NLP) and sentiment analysis; 11. Hands-on implementation using Python libraries (scikit-learn, matplotlib, plotly, pandas, textblob); 12. Overview of sustainability research: leading journals, research areas, key concepts, and examples; 13. Development of a final project applying models to real-world problems in operations management and sustainability.
Type of assessment:
Assessment will be based on Project-Based Learning (PBL),
(a) Practical case study assignments developed at the end of each class (40% of the final grade), involving individual or group activities with data analysis, modeling, computational implementation, interpretation of results, and critical discussion; and
(b) a final mini paper (60% of the final grade), involving the application of machine learning models to a real-world problem related to sustainability, operations management, or related fields.
Bibliography: JAMES, G.; WITTEN, D.; HASTIE, T.; TIBSHIRANI, R.; TAYLOR, J. An Introduction to Statistical Learning: with Applications in Python. New York: Springer, 2023.

Name: Applied Solutions for Water Security
Professors: Eduardo Mario Mendiondo
Objectives: Introduce concepts and applications of water security relating traditional and novel approaches in water engineering sciences. Evaluate viable strategies of water security adapting novel design and innovation solutions for a wide range of problems at local, regional, national and international levels. Coach future water leaders through practical cases studies of water security, using past and running research and development projects, with international frameworks and standards.
Rationale: Water security is an interdisciplinary and multi-dimensional concept, which assesses levels of hydrological risks considered tolerable for a society under change. Also, water security reaches a variety of multipurpose water resources and uses, both consumptive and nonconsumptive, which affect sustainability in general. However, this concept is rapidly evolving with recent emergence of human demands of climate-resilient mechanisms, sustainable development goals and social engagement to cope with disaster risks in the actural Anthropocene's moment. Approximately 50% of Brazilian population and ca. 60% of world population will suffer with water insecurity until year 2020. More than two thirds of global wealth is under compromise with the security of the nexus water-food-energy. Thus, both integrated and applied approaches on water security are necessary to assist the new generation of water leaders and decision makers to wisely address innovative solutions in a wide range of possibilities of economic, social and environmental issues. The selected syllabi and dynamics of classes of this new course were specially prepared to help with those challenges using: (1) updated references, books, reports and papers to assist classes and practical assignments, (2) an international network of leading institutions and specialists on water security to collaborate with lessons learned from regional and local scales, and (3) own experiences throughout 25 years of national and international participation in water resources enginering projects. Special focus of the Course will be addressed from the running National Science & Technlogy Institute (INCT) on Climate Change, Water Security Component (2016-2020), with support of CNPq and FAPESP.

Name: Solar Water Technologies and Advanced Oxidation Processes for Water Treatment and Wastewater Reuse
Professors: Lyda Patricia Sabogal Paz
Objectives:
The overall aim of this course is to equip postgraduate students with an advanced, integrated understanding of solar-driven water treatment systems and advanced oxidation processes, enabling them to design, evaluate, and critically assess sustainable water and wastewater treatment solutions, particularly in the context of water reuse and resource-constrained environments. By the end of the course, students should be able to:
1.	Explain the scientific principles of solar water technologies, including solar disinfection (SODIS), solar photocatalysis, and solar-driven desalination systems.
2.	Describe the chemistry and physics of advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), including radical generation mechanisms (�OH, SO₄�⁻) and their role in contaminant degradation.
3.	Differentiate between key AOP technologies, such as: UV/H₂O₂; Ozonation and peroxone; Fenton and photo-Fenton processes; Photocatalysis and electrochemical oxidation.
4.	Critically evaluate the suitability of solar technologies and AOPs for different water matrices (e.g., drinking water, industrial wastewater, greywater).
5.	Analyse transformation pathways and by-product formation during AOP treatment and assess associated environmental and health risks.
6.	Understand the limitations of conventional water treatment processes and the role of solar and AOP systems in addressing emerging contaminants and pathogens. 
7.	Explain the principles of wastewater reuse and water circularity, including regulatory drivers and sustainability considerations.
8.	Compare treatment options based on performance, energy demand, cost, and scalability, including rural and low-resource settings.
9.	Assess the applicability and risks of solar-powered water systems in real-world contexts, including humanitarian and development scenarios. 
10.	Interpret experimental and field data from AOP and solar treatment systems.
Rationale: The growing need for hydraulic, civil, chemical and environmental engineering graduates to engage with advanced, sustainable water treatment technologies that extend beyond conventional infrastructure design is a reality. As water systems face increasing pressures from climate change, scarcity, and the presence of emerging contaminants, engineers must integrate treatment processes such as solar-driven systems and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) into the planning and design of resilient infrastructure. AOPs are essential for achieving stringent water quality standards and addressing contaminants that conventional methods cannot effectively remove, while solar technologies offer low-energy, decentralised solutions particularly suited to resource-constrained and climate-vulnerable contexts. For postgraduate students, this course provides critical interdisciplinary competencies at the interface of water treatment, process engineering, and sustainability, enabling them to design integrated water reuse systems, critically evaluate emerging technologies, and contribute to the transition toward circular and climate-resilient water infrastructure.
Content:
1.	Fundamentals of water quality and emerging contaminants
1.1.	Existing pollutants
1.2.	Emerging and challenging water pollutants
1.3.	Water quality monitoring
1.4.	Water quality indexes
2.	Solar water technologies (SODIS, solar photocatalysis, solar reactors)
2.1.	Solar disinfection
2.2.	Solar heterogeneous photocatalysis
2.3.	Solar homogeneous photocatalysis
2.4.	Solar photo-reactors for water remediation
3.	Advanced oxidation processes 
3.1.	UV/H₂O₂
3.2.	ozone-based
3.3.	Fenton and Photo-Fenton
3.4.	Photocatalysis
3.5.	Electrocatalysis
3.6.	Photoelectrocatalysis
4.	Reactor design 
4.1	Mass transfer
4.2	Light adsorption
4.3	Quantum yield
4.4	Adsorption/desorption 
4.5	Key reactor parameters as per AOP
5.	Integration with wastewater treatment and reuse systems
5.1	Existing technologies WWTPs
5.2	Innovative WW treatment and reuse technologies
5.3	Risks and regulations associated to reclaimed water regulation
6.	Case studies in decentralised and global water systems
6.1	Case studies in Europe
6.2	Case studies in Brazil
6.3	Case studies in the rest of the world.
Type of assessment: Written work related to a relevant research paper provided to the students at the beginning of the course (100% of the grade)

Name: Cheminformatics & Drug Discovery with Machine Learning
Professors:
Carlos Alberto Montanari
Andrei Leit�o
Objectives: We provide the fundamental concepts in cheminformatics and artificial intelligence with machine learning in moleculardesign aided by computer. Data mining to obtain information from the chemical-biological space and extract knowledgefrom large and complex collections of data. Apply these concepts for the prediction of bioactive new chemical entities (NCE) from known, virtual and in silico chemical substances.
Rationale: Cheminformatics is a field of chemistry that uses computational chemistry, which is currently associated with machine learning, to solve chemically-driven problems to describe how drugs act in biological systems. In this discipline, we will address artificial intelligence applications with machine learning for the molecular design of bioactive new chemical entities (NCE). The main focus will be based on currently available data to grasp relations between data andinformation, to get patterns from information to knowledge, to learn from the experience to understanding thephenomenon under scrutiny. Moreover, the focus will be on the translation from previous experience (pastcomprehension) to wisdom, thereby to guide the decision-making process (future application) necessary to the inherentchallenges of computational prediction of the physiological context (in vivo). Learning to connect multivariate relationships between data and principles will help decide how to transform a chemical entity into a NCE. The emblematic challenging problems of converting 2D chemical structures into 3D ones that are suitable for machine learning models,including the chemical-biological space to gain a better understanding of which molecular characteristics, are essential forthe desired predictions, and it constitutes an interface that will be addressed in this discipline.
Content:
1. A brief history of cheminformatics and the lattest applications
2. Representa��o das estruturas qu�micas 2D em computador
3. Characterization of 2D structures using descriptors and fingerprints
4. Storage and use of 2D representations to search dababanks
5. Como representar rea��es qu�micas no computador para a constru��o de cole��es virtuais de subst�ncias qu�micas
6. How to represent the 3D chemical stuctures
7. Public domein databanks
8. Supervised and unsupervised learning
9. Structure-property relationships (SPR)
10. Structure-activity relationships (SAR)
11. Structure-toxicidity relationships (STR)
12. Application of KNIME for data analysis and machine learning
Type of assessment: Two group activities and one final test ith a Project of a nvel bioactive chemical discovery will have equal weight to atribute the final score.

Name: Introduction to Computational Methods for Protein Studies
Professors: Adriano Marques Gon�alves
Objectives: At the end of the course, it is expected that students understand concepts and techniques that assist in the use of computational methodologies in the study of proteins and other macromolecules, including the use and interpretation of databases, protein structure prediction, and molecular docking. Additionally, students should be able to recognize the various fields of application of the covered computational methodologies.
Rationale: Computational tools have held a prominent position in research, being able to substantially contribute to research projects in various fields. However, the mastery and use of these tools are still limited. In this regard, the current course aims to introduce the possibilities of work and its applications to graduate students. Currently, there are servers and online tools that allow access to various methodologies in a democratic manner, without the need to have knowledge of any programming language or access to very powerful computers. Therefore, it is possible, initially, to carry out computational work by using low-cost computational  tools. From this point on, students can reflect on their research, with the possibility of incorporating the methodologies covered in the course.
Content: 1) Protein Structure and Function; 2) Sequence Databases (UniProt) and Protein Structures (PDB); 3) Protein Structure Prediction using Online Tools (Swiss-Model and AlphaFold2) and Model Quality Assessment; 4) Ligand Construction (Avogadro 2); 5) Molecular Docking of Ligands to Proteins as a Tool for Health Studies, using an Online Server (Swiss-Dock) and Result Analysis (Discovery Studio Visualizer); 6) Utilization of Online Tools: UniProt, PDB, Swiss-Model, AlphaFold2, and Swiss-Dock; 7) Utilization of Free Access Software: Discovery Studio Visualizer and Avogadro 2.

Name: Feed Formulation for Pigs
Professors: Cesar Augusto Pospissil Garbossa
Objectives: Enable the student to understand the nutritional requirements, possible ingredients and nutritional restrictions, the composition of feed and formulate minimum cost feeds for pigs, knowing and using computer programs for the different categories studied.
Rationale: In recent years the production of swine in Brazil and in the world has grown sharply. This growth has been accompanied by the constant work carried out by geneticists. However, for the animal to express its full genetic potential, adequate nutrition is necessary in order to meet its needs. Therefore, the formulation of diets that provide nutrients for the animal category is an important factor for the training of professionals who will work in the area of animal production, knowing the basic points and all the challenges that are inherent to the formulation process.
Content:
Nutritional requirements of pigs
Composition and classification of the main foods used in swine feeding.
Food restriction and anti-nutritional factors
Feed formulation following the different methodologies
Type of assessment: Forty percent of the grade will be related to the presentation of seminars during the semester; 40% will refer to the evaluation and 20% will refer to participation during classes. Considering: A � Excellent, entitled to credit; B � Good, entitled to credit; C � Regular, entitled to credit; R � Failed, without credit.

Name: Special Topics in Animal Nutrition and Production - III
Professors: Cesar Augusto Pospissil Garbossa
Objectives: To address topics or content not included in the curricular structure of the Graduate Program in Animal Nutrition and Production, but that are scientifically relevant to the field. This course should be primarily used when hosting visiting professors from foreign institutions or other institutions in Brazil.
Rationale: The constant evolution of scientific and technological knowledge in the field of Animal Nutrition and Production requires Graduate Programs to remain up to date and open to the incorporation of new topics and approaches. In this context, this course was designed to provide a flexible academic structure that allows for the inclusion of emerging, interdisciplinary, or highly specialized topics which, although not part of the regular curriculum of the Program, have significant scientific relevance and the potential to contribute meaningfully to the students� education. The proposal is particularly relevant in situations involving visits by renowned professors and researchers from foreign institutions or other Brazilian universities, whose expertise can enrich the academic environment and broaden students� perspectives. By enabling the timely offering of innovative and current content, this course also fosters the exchange of experiences, the internationalization of the Program, and the strengthening of scientific collaboration networks.
Content: This course aims to address topics or content that are not part of the curricular structure of the Graduate Program in Animal Nutrition and Production but are scientifically relevant to the field. It should be primarily offered when hosting visiting professors from foreign institutions or other institutions in Brazil.
Type of assessment: When requesting the offering of such a course, the proposing faculty member must present the assessment method that will be used. The final academic classification will correspond to the following ranges: 10-9 (A); 8,9-7 (B); 6,9-5 (C); 4,9-0 (R).
